    "thinking": " The user wants Multi-Watercolor v0.2 to start with a blank canvas and no UI elements visible, letting them draw immediately with mouse or touch input using the library's built-in pointer event support.\n\nNow I'm reconsidering what \"draw with no UI to start\" actually means \u2014 it could be a fresh canvas for painting, or it could mean keeping the thumbnails visible but enabling drawing on the hero canvas itself. The challenge is that in v0.1, the hero canvas has pointer events disabled so clicks pass through to the thumbnails below, so I'd need to either enable pointer events on the hero (which would block thumbnail interaction), add selective pointer handling that avoids the thumbnail areas, or implement a mode toggle to switch between drawing and browsing.\n\nThe cleanest approach is to enable pointer events on the hero canvas and rely on the cards sitting above it with their own click handlers \u2014 as long as those handlers call `stopPropagation()`, the pointer events won't bubble down to trigger painting when clicking a card. The real issue is that the library's pointer listeners are attached directly to the canvas element, so I need to make sure the card interactions don't interfere with the hero's ability to detect and respond to painting gestures. above (`z-index: 5` vs hero's `z-index: 1`), pointer events on the cards would target the cards first, not the canvas. So pointer events on the canvas only fire when the user is NOT over a card.\n\nThat should work natively with the DOM event system. Let me verify:\n\nWhen the user mouses over a card, pointer events target the card (because cards are above in stacking order). When mousing over empty hero area, events target the canvas. So if I just enable `pointer: true` on hero, and keep cards with their click handlers, the user can:\n- Paint on the hero (when not over a card)\n- Click a card to trigger obliterate\n\nThat's exactly the right behavior. So v0.2 is essentially v0.1 with `pointer: true` on hero (instead of `pointer: false`).\n\nBy \"no ui to start,\" the user probably means no control panels or chrome like the main app has, though there's already minimal UI with the header and footer. To implement drawing without extra controls, I'd enable pointer events on the hero canvas and rely on the z-index layering to keep card interactions separate. For affordance, I could add a subtle hint about dragging to paint, and I'm considering whether to include brush controls or keep it simple with a single fixed brush style. Looking at the user's request more carefully, I think the right approach is to keep the thumbnails visible like in v0.1 but make the hero canvas immediately drawable without any UI clutter\u2014just a clean, minimal interface where painting is the obvious first action. I'll set up a default brush with a rainbow color cycle and medium size for intuitive finger painting, then add a subtle hint that disappears after the first stroke. The hero canvas brush should stay completely isolated from the thumbnail instances, and I'm thinking through how the cursor preview will work alongside the drawing interaction. the hero \u2014 should it show? The hero is a \"playful surface\" so yes, the cursor preview helps. But on touch devices we don't want it.\n\nCurrently v0.1 has `cursorPreview: false` on hero. I'll change to `cursorPreview: true` so desktop users see the brush; on touch devices the lib auto-disables it anyway via mobile detection.\n\nWait, the cursor preview in the lib is a flag, but the actual cursor element is in the wiring HTML. In multi-watercolor, there's no separate cursor element \u2014 we'd need to build one OR rely on browser cursor. The wiring manages a single shared cursor element, but multi-watercolor doesn't have one, so `cursorPreview: true` wouldn't actually do anything functional.\n\nFor v0.2, I think I'll keep it simple and skip the cursor preview entirely \u2014 just enable pointer interaction and let the browser handle the default cursor. I could implement a custom cursor inline, but that's extra complexity for a minimal demo. Instead, I'll set up an explicit brush state with a nice color like rainbow to give users something visually interesting to paint with.\n\nActually, rainbow feels gimmicky \u2014 better to pick a single appealing pigment or let users cycle through them with keyboard shortcuts. I'll go with a minimal approach: enable pointer, use the default rose pigment from create(), add a small instruction label, and wire up keyboard shortcuts (1/2/3 for pigment cycling, R for reset) without any visible UI buttons.\n\nFor the \"no UI\" requirement, I should strip away the demo header and footer from v0.1 entirely \u2014 just the hero canvas with thumbnail cards on top. To help users discover they can paint, I'll add a brief tooltip that appears on first load, says \"draw with mouse or finger,\" and fades after a few seconds or on first interaction.\n\nI'll also add keyboard shortcuts for switching pigments (1/2/3) and resetting (R), but skip the hidden options panel to keep things minimal. -->\n<script>\n\"use strict\";\n\n// ----------------------------------------------------------------------\n// SVG sources \u2014 unchanged from v0.1.\n// ----------------------------------------------------------------------\nconst SVGS = {\n  orchard:\n    '<svg viewBox=\"0 0 100 100\" xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\">' +\n      '<path d=\"M 50 85 L 50 50\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 50 60 L 35 45\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 50 55 L 65 40\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 30 35 C 25 25, 40 18, 50 25 C 60 18, 75 25, 70 35 C 75 45, 60 48, 50 40 C 40 48, 25 45, 30 35 Z\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 10 85 L 90 85\"/>' +\n    '</svg>',\n  harbor:\n    '<svg viewBox=\"0 0 100 100\" xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\">' +\n      '<path d=\"M 75 25 m -10 0 a 10 10 0 1 0 20 0 a 10 10 0 1 0 -20 0\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 25 65 Q 50 75 70 65 L 65 60 L 30 60 Z\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 47 60 L 47 30\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 47 30 L 47 55 L 62 55 Z\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 10 75 Q 20 73 30 75 T 50 75 T 70 75 T 90 75\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 10 82 Q 20 80 30 82 T 50 82 T 70 82 T 90 82\"/>' +\n    '</svg>',\n  meadow:\n    '<svg viewBox=\"0 0 100 100\" xmlns=\"http://www.w3.org/2000/svg\">' +\n      '<path d=\"M 25 25 m -8 0 a 8 8 0 1 0 16 0 a 8 8 0 1 0 -16 0\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 0 65 Q 30 55 55 60 Q 80 65 100 58\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 0 80 Q 25 72 50 78 Q 75 84 100 74\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 30 90 L 30 78\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 50 92 L 50 80\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 70 91 L 70 79\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 30 77 m -2 0 a 2 2 0 1 0 4 0 a 2 2 0 1 0 -4 0\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 50 79 m -2 0 a 2 2 0 1 0 4 0 a 2 2 0 1 0 -4 0\"/>' +\n      '<path d=\"M 70 78 m -2 0 a 2 2 0 1 0 4 0 a 2 2 0 1 0 -4 0\"/>' +\n    '</svg>',\n};\n\nfunction boot() {\n  if (typeof window.Watercolor === 'undefined') {\n    return setTimeout(boot, 30);\n  }\n\n  // ------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // HERO \u2014 full-viewport, NOW PAINTABLE.\n  // ------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // The key difference from v0.1: `pointer: true` enables the lib's\n  // pointer handlers on the hero canvas, so mouse / touch / pen drag\n  // events become paint strokes. The thumbnail cards still sit above\n  // in z-order, so DOM event routing handles the \"click on a card =\n  // navigate, click on background = paint\" decision for free.\n  //\n  // No animation is set this time \u2014 the canvas starts blank. The user\n  // is the animation. (If you want a soft running background instead,\n  // call hero.setAnimation('partlyCloudy') here; the painting and the\n  // animation will compose.)\n  const hero = window.Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('hero'), {\n    canvasScale: 0.6,\n    pointer: true,            // \u2190 v0.2: drawable\n    cursorPreview: false,     // hero uses native crosshair (set in CSS)\n                              // \u2014 no DOM cursor preview, keeping the\n                              //   demo's no-UI promise.\n  });\n  // Tweak default brush state for the multi-canvas context. The lib's\n  // defaults are tuned for a focused single-canvas painting app; on\n  // a portfolio-style background, a slightly bigger, slightly drier\n  // brush feels nicer for casual finger painting.\n  hero.brushSize(60);\n  hero.paintLoad(0.85);\n  hero.waterLoad(1.1);\n  hero.pigment('rose');\n\n  // ------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // THUMBNAILS \u2014 unchanged from v0.1.\n  // ------------------------------------------------------------------\n  const thumbConfigs = [\n    { hostId: 'thumb-1', project: 'orchard', pigment: 'blue'   },\n    { hostId: 'thumb-2', project: 'harbor',  pigment: 'rose'   },\n    { hostId: 'thumb-3', project: 'meadow',  pigment: 'yellow' },\n  ];\n  const thumbInstances = thumbConfigs.map((cfg, i) => {\n    const hostEl = document.getElementById(cfg.hostId);\n    const wc = window.Watercolor.create(hostEl, {\n      canvasScale: 0.7,\n      pointer: false,\n      cursorPreview: false,\n    });\n    wc.sketchMode();\n    setTimeout(() => {\n      wc.traceSVG(SVGS[cfg.project], {\n        pigment: cfg.pigment,\n        durationMs: 1500,\n        easing: 'penStroke',\n        perStrokePauseMs: 200,\n      });\n    }, 250 + i * 350);\n    return { ...cfg, wc, hostEl };\n  });\n\n  thumbInstances.forEach((thumb) => {\n    const card = thumb.hostEl.closest('.thumb');\n    if (!card) return;\n    let busy = false;\n    card.addEventListener('click', async (e) => {\n      if (busy) return;\n      busy = true;\n      const rect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\n      const grid = thumb.wc.toGrid(e.clientX - rect.left, e.clientY - rect.top);\n      await thumb.wc.obliterate({\n        mode: 'water', durationMs: 500,\n        x: grid.gx, y: grid.gy,\n      });\n      console.log('Project clicked:', thumb.project,\n                  '\u2014 v2 would navigate here.');\n      setTimeout(() => {\n        thumb.wc.reset();\n        thumb.wc.sketchMode();\n        thumb.wc.traceSVG(SVGS[thumb.project], {\n          pigment: thumb.pigment,\n          durationMs: 1200,\n          easing: 'penStroke',\n          perStrokePauseMs: 150,\n        });\n        busy = false;\n      }, 900);\n    });\n  });\n\n  // ------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // FIRST-PAINT HINT \u2014 fades on first paint OR after 4 seconds.\n  // ------------------------------------------------------------------\n  const hint = document.getElementById('hint');\n  let hintGone = false;\n  function dismissHint() {\n    if (hintGone || !hint) return;\n    hintGone = true;\n    hint.classList.add('gone');\n    // Remove from layout after the fade completes so it doesn't catch\n    // accidental focus / screen-reader noise.\n    setTimeout(() => { if (hint.parentNode) hint.parentNode.removeChild(hint); }, 800);\n  }\n  // Fade after 4 seconds even if the user never paints.\n  setTimeout(dismissHint, 4000);\n  // Or fade the instant any paint happens on the hero. The hero\n  // canvas's pointerdown is the cleanest signal \u2014 we don't care\n  // whether the user actually drew anything, just whether they\n  // started interacting.\n  const heroEl = document.getElementById('hero');\n  heroEl.addEventListener('pointerdown', dismissHint, { once: true });\n\n  // ------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // KEYBOARD SHORTCUTS \u2014 the only \"UI\" in v0.2.\n  // ------------------------------------------------------------------\n  // Numbers 1\u20134 switch the hero brush; R resets; H toggles the help.\n  // Match the main app's convention: H keypress in non-input context.\n  const helpEl = document.getElementById('help');\n  function toggleHelp() {\n    if (!helpEl) return;\n    const open = helpEl.classList.toggle('open');\n    helpEl.setAttribute('aria-hidden', String(!open));\n  }\n  document.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {\n    // Ignore key events targeting form inputs (defensive \u2014 there are\n    // none in v0.2, but future additions might add them).\n    if (e.target && (e.target.tagName === 'INPUT' || e.target.tagName === 'TEXTAREA')) return;\n    const k = e.key;\n    if (k === '1') { hero.pigment('rose');   dismissHint(); }\n    else if (k === '2') { hero.pigment('yellow'); dismissHint(); }\n    else if (k === '3') { hero.pigment('blue');   dismissHint(); }\n    else if (k === '4') { hero.pigment('water');  dismissHint(); }\n    else if (k === 'r' || k === 'R') { hero.reset(); dismissHint(); }\n    else if (k === 'h' || k === 'H') { toggleHelp(); }\n    else if (k === 'Escape') {\n      if (helpEl.classList.contains('open')) toggleHelp();\n    }\n  });\n  // Clicking outside the help overlay closes it.\n  document.addEventListener('pointerdown', (e) => {\n    if (!helpEl.classList.contains('open')) return;\n    if (helpEl.contains(e.target)) return;\n    toggleHelp();\n  });\n}\n\nif (document.readyState === 'loading') {\n  document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', boot);\n} else {\n  boot();\n}\n</script>\n\n</body>\n</html>\n",

The lib's\\n  // defaults are tuned for a focused single-canvas painting app; on\\n  // a portfolio-style background, a slightly bigger, slightly drier\\n  // brush feels nicer for casual finger painting.\\n  hero.brushSize(60);\\n  hero.paintLoad(0.85);\\n  hero.waterLoad(1.1);\\n  hero.pigment('rose');\\n\\n  // ------------------------------------------------------------------\\n  // THUMBNAILS \\u2014 unchanged from v0.1.\\n  // ------------------------------------------------------------------\\n  const thumbConfigs = [\\n    { hostId: 'thumb-1', project: 'orchard', pigment: 'blue'   },\\n    { hostId: 'thumb-2', project: 'harbor',  pigment: 'rose'   },\\n    { hostId: 'thumb-3', project: 'meadow',  pigment: 'yellow' },\\n  ];\\n  const thumbInstances = thumbConfigs.map((cfg, i) => {\\n    const hostEl = document.getElementById(cfg.hostId);\\n    const wc = window.Watercolor.create(hostEl, {\\n      canvasScale: 0.7,\\n      pointer: false,\\n      cursorPreview: false,\\n    });\\n    wc.sketchMode();\\n    setTimeout(() => {\\n      wc.traceSVG(SVGS[cfg.project], {\\n        pigment: cfg.pigment,\\n        durationMs: 1500,\\n        easing: 'penStroke',\\n        perStrokePauseMs: 200,\\n      });\\n    }, 250 + i * 350);\\n    return { ...cfg, wc, hostEl };\\n  });\\n\\n  thumbInstances.forEach((thumb) => {\\n    const card = thumb.hostEl.closest('.thumb');\\n    if (!card) return;\\n    let busy = false;\\n    card.addEventListener('click', async (e) => {\\n      if (busy) return;\\n      busy = true;\\n      const rect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\\n      const grid = thumb.wc.toGrid(e.clientX - rect.left, e.clientY - rect.top);\\n      await thumb.wc.obliterate({\\n        mode: 'water', durationMs: 500,\\n        x: grid.gx, y: grid.gy,\\n      });\\n      console.log('Project clicked:', thumb.project,\\n                  '\\u2014 v2 would navigate here.');\\n      setTimeout(() => {\\n        thumb.wc.reset();\\n        thumb.wc.sketchMode();\\n        thumb.wc.traceSVG(SVGS[thumb.project], {\\n          pigment: thumb.pigment,\\n          durationMs: 1200,\\n          easing: 'penStroke',\\n          perStrokePauseMs: 150,\\n        });\\n        busy = false;\\n      }, 900);\\n    });\\n  });\\n\\n  // ------------------------------------------------------------------\\n  // FIRST-PAINT HINT \\u2014 fades on first paint OR after 4 seconds.\\n  // ------------------------------------------------------------------\\n  const hint = document.getElementById('hint');\\n  let hintGone = false;\\n  function dismissHint() {\\n    if (hintGone || !hint) return;\\n    hintGone = true;\\n    hint.classList.add('gone');\\n    // Remove from layout after the fade completes so it doesn't catch\\n    // accidental focus / screen-reader noise.\\n    setTimeout(() => { if (hint.parentNode) hint.parentNode.removeChild(hint); }, 800);\\n  }\\n  // Fade after 4 seconds even if the user never paints.\\n  setTimeout(dismissHint, 4000);\\n  // Or fade the instant any paint happens on the hero. R resets; H toggles the help.\\n  // Match the main app's convention: H keypress in non-input context.\\n  const helpEl = document.getElementById('help');\\n  function toggleHelp() {\\n    if (!helpEl) return;\\n    const open = helpEl.classList.toggle('open');\\n    helpEl.setAttribute('aria-hidden', String(!open));\\n  }\\n  document.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {\\n    // Ignore key events targeting form inputs (defensive \\u2014 there are\\n    // none in v0.2, but future additions might add them).\\n    if (e.target && (e.target.tagName === 'INPUT' || e.target.tagName === 'TEXTAREA')) return;\\n    const k = e.key;\\n    if (k === '1') { hero.pigment('rose');   dismissHint(); }\\n    else if (k === '2') { hero.pigment('yellow'); dismissHint(); }\\n    else if (k === '3') { hero.pigment('blue');   dismissHint(); }\\n    else if (k === '4') { hero.pigment('water');     "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**Multi-Watercolor v0.2 shipped.** Hero is now paintable while staying visually unchromed.\n\n## What changed from v0.1\n\n**One library option flip on the hero:**\n\n```js\nconst hero = window.Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('hero'), {\n  canvasScale: 0.6,\n  pointer: true,            // \u2190 v0.2: was false in v0.1\n  cursorPreview: false,\n});\n```\n\n`pointer: true` activates the library's built-in pointermove/pointerdown/pointerup handlers. The card thumbnails sit above the hero in z-order (`.thumb { z-index: 5 }` vs `.hero { z-index: 1 }`), so DOM event routing handles the disambiguation for free \u2014 pointer events over a card target the card and trigger its click handler; events over empty hero area target the canvas and the lib paints. No event-coordination code needed.\n\n**Plus a few hero brush defaults** that feel right for a background-canvas painting context (slightly larger brush, slightly drier paint, slightly more water for nice diffusion):\n\n```js\nhero.brushSize(60);\nhero.paintLoad(0.85);\nhero.waterLoad(1.1);\nhero.pigment('rose');\n```\n\nThe lib's defaults are tuned for a focused painting app. On a portfolio background where users will doodle casually with a finger or mouse, a bigger, looser brush feels more inviting.\n\n## The no-UI promise\n\nI removed the demo header and footer from v0.1 entirely. The page now contains:\n\n1. The hero canvas\n2. The three thumbnail cards\n3. A small ephemeral hint that says **\"draw anywhere \u00b7 press H for shortcuts\"**\n\nThe hint auto-dismisses on the first `pointerdown` on the hero OR after 4 seconds \u2014 whichever comes first. After that, the page is fully clean. Just the painted surface and the cards.\n\nFor interaction without buttons:\n\n```\n1 / 2 / 3   \u2192 rose / yellow / blue brush\n4           \u2192 water brush (lifts pigment)\nR           \u2192 reset hero canvas\nH           \u2192 toggle help overlay\nEsc         \u2192 close help\n```\n\nThe help overlay (`H`) is the only \"UI\" and it's keyboard-summoned only. By default it's `pointer-events: none`, `opacity: 0` \u2014 not just hidden, completely inert. Clicking outside it (when open) also closes it.\n\n## Why CSS `touch-action: none` matters here\n\nAdded to `html, body`:\n\n```css\ntouch-action: none;\n-webkit-user-select: none;\nuser-select: none;\n```\n\nOn a touch device, the browser by default consumes drag gestures for scrolling and pinch-zooming, never delivering them to JS. With `touch-action: none`, all touch events route to the canvas's pointer handlers \u2014 which is what you want for a drawing surface. Without it, finger-painting on mobile silently scrolls the page instead of leaving paint marks.\n\n`overflow: hidden` + `overscroll-behavior: none` round out the \"this is a drawing app, not a document\" feel.\n\n## Cursor affordance\n\nThe hero uses the native `cursor: crosshair` from CSS \u2014 no DOM cursor preview element to maintain. Decision: a DOM cursor preview would have been more thematic but would have required either (a) including the cursor-preview wiring code from the main app (a fair chunk of code for what's meant to be a minimal demo) or (b) building a stripped-down version. Native crosshair is the right call for v0.2 \u2014 it tells the user \"this is a drawing surface\" with one line of CSS. Future v0.3 could add the themed cursor if you want.\n\n**Verified in Node:** hero accepts custom brush state after create, all four pigment shortcuts route correctly through `pigment('rose'|'yellow'|'blue'|'water')`, reset clears state, instance isolation from v0.1 still holds (thumb instances unaffected by hero changes).\n\nThe only thing I couldn't verify in headless Node is the actual pointer-painting (no real pointer events in the stub) \u2014 but the library has been painting via the same `pointer: true` code path since v0.1 of the lib, so that's a known-good code path.",
    "citations": []
  }

    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.3 shipped.** Both fixes plus a confession.\n\n## The pointer-position \"feature\" was actually broken since v0.1\n\nI want to flag this clearly because I wrote that code and called it working in three separate ship messages. It wasn't. Two bugs stacked on top of each other made every click obliterate at canvas center, regardless of where you clicked:\n\n**Bug 1 \u2014 double subtraction.** The library's `toGrid()` already calls `canvas.getBoundingClientRect()` and subtracts the canvas's `left/top` internally:\n\n```js\n// inside the lib:\ntoGrid(displayX, displayY) {\n  const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();\n  return {\n    x: (displayX - rect.left) * (GW / rect.width),\n    y: (displayY - rect.top)  * (GH / rect.height),\n  };\n}\n```\n\nMy multi-instance code was pre-subtracting the *card's* rect:\n\n```js\nconst rect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\nconst grid = thumb.wc.toGrid(e.clientX - rect.left, e.clientY - rect.top);\n```\n\nSo the lib received already-relative coordinates and subtracted again. Result: x/y values that were arbitrary offsets in negative or wrong-magnitude space.\n\n**Bug 2 \u2014 wrong destructuring property names.** `toGrid` returns `{ x, y }`. My code read `{ gx, gy }`:\n\n```js\nawait thumb.wc.obliterate({ x: grid.gx, y: grid.gy });\n```\n\nBoth destructured to `undefined`. The lib's `obliterate` then ran:\n\n```js\nconst cx = opts.x !== undefined ? +opts.x : GW * 0.5;\nconst cy = opts.y !== undefined ? +opts.y : GH * 0.5;\n```\n\n`opts.x === undefined` \u2192 fell through to `GW * 0.5`. Always canvas center. *Every single click.*\n\nI had verified the call shape in my Node tests (which I noted \"didn't exercise actual pointer events\") and missed this in v0.1, v0.2, *and* in the agents-recipe documentation I wrote for the main app. Worth fixing in the docs too \u2014 flagging that for the next time we touch the main HTML.\n\nConfirmed via headless test that the new code path returns the correct grid coords (center click on a 132\u00d7132 grid \u2192 `{x: 66, y: 66}`).\n\n**The fix:**\n\n```js\nconst { x, y } = thumb.wc.toGrid(e.clientX, e.clientY);\nawait thumb.wc.obliterate({ mode: 'water', durationMs: 500, x, y });\n```\n\nThree lines. Now obliterate centers exactly where the click happened.\n\n## Thumbnails fill width\n\nOn both layouts:\n\n**Desktop (column):** container caps at `min(440px, calc(100vw - 48px))`. Cards span 100% of that with `aspect-ratio: 1` so they stay square. Compared to v0.1/v0.2's fixed `220px \u00d7 220px`, cards are now almost double the size on a typical desktop. There's also a `max-height: 30vh` defensive cap so 3 cards \u00d7 30vh + 2 \u00d7 18px gap = ~96vh, never overflowing the viewport even on short windows.\n\n**Mobile (row):** container spans `left: 0; right: 0; padding: 0 12px` \u2014 full viewport width minus 12px on each side. Each card uses `flex: 1` so the three cards split the available width equally. `aspect-ratio: 1` inherited from the desktop rule keeps them square. On a 375px iPhone viewport, each card becomes ~117px (was 100px); on iPad portrait ~257px each.\n\nBoth layouts use `aspect-ratio: 1`, so the same SVG sketches scale up cleanly \u2014 no layout-shift visible at the breakpoint other than the desktop\u2192mobile flex-direction flip.\n\n**Retired** `multi-watercolor-v0.1.html` and `multi-watercolor-v0.2.html` from the outputs directory since both had the pointer-position bug. v0.3 supersedes them entirely.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.4 shipped.** Both features added; the no-UI character of the page is preserved.\n\n## Random scene on load\n\nCurated pools, picked one of each at boot, applied to the hero:\n\n```js\nconst ANIMATIONS  = ['rainy', 'sunny', 'windy', 'partlyCloudy',\n                     'snowing', 'snowingAdditive', 'ai'];\nconst BACKGROUNDS = ['day', 'dawn', 'sunset', 'night'];\nconst chosenAnim = pickRandom(ANIMATIONS);\nconst chosenBg   = pickRandom(BACKGROUNDS);\nhero.setBackground(chosenBg);   // base wash first\nhero.setAnimation(chosenAnim);  // animation on top\n```\n\n**Why the order matters:** `setBackground` paints a time-of-day wash across the canvas \u2014 it's a one-shot brush operation. `setAnimation` registers a continuous per-frame step function. If you reverse the order, the animation paints briefly, then the background wash covers it. Background first \u2192 wash settles in \u2192 animation builds on top.\n\n**Curation rationale.** I left out `thunderstorm` and `tornado` from the animation pool. Both are great intentional choices but jarring as a default first-load impression \u2014 flashing lightning or chaotic swirling pigment isn't what you want a portfolio visitor to land on before they've had a chance to look around. `tornado` is also a background option but I left it out of the background pool for the same reason. `'ai'` is a real animation mode (gentle painterly exploration) and kept in.\n\nThat gives **28 unique combinations** (7 animations \u00d7 4 backgrounds). Verified all 28 accept-without-throw in Node; verified 1000 random picks cover all combinations roughly uniformly.\n\n## Perf overlay \u2014 `P` to toggle\n\nThe lib gives me `perf(on)` to enable instrumentation and `perfMetrics()` to read the numbers; display is up to the host. Built a minimal overlay in the top-right corner that polls at 4 Hz (250ms \u2014 same rate as the main app):\n\n```\nSCENE\nfps         60.2\nframe p50   3.42ms\np95         8.11ms\np99         12.4ms\nsim         1.20ms\nrender      1.85ms\nwash        0.31ms\nactive      14.2%\nheap \u0394      2.3MB\nloaf        0\n```\n\nThe scene title at the top shows `<animation> \u00b7 <background>` so you can correlate the readout to what's running \u2014 FPS at `tornado` isn't the same story as FPS at `partlyCloudy`. Only the hero is instrumented (it's the only one that runs continuously; thumbnails idle out after their trace).\n\n**Detail worth flagging:** the perf instrumentation has measurable overhead \u2014 the lib gates `performance.now()` calls behind `perfEnabled`. With perf off, those calls don't happen. So toggling perf on actually changes what you're measuring, which is its own kind of Heisenberg situation. For a portfolio page this doesn't matter; for actual perf hunting you'd want to leave it on for the whole session.\n\nWhen you toggle off (`P` again), the polling interval is cleared so the page returns to fully idle (modulo the hero animation's own RAF).\n\n## Updated hint and help\n\nThe first-paint hint now reads: **`draw anywhere \u00b7 H for shortcuts \u00b7 P for perf`** so both keystrokes are discoverable without opening the help overlay. The help overlay itself has a new `P  Toggle perf overlay` row.\n\n## Total keystrokes in v0.4\n\n```\n1 / 2 / 3   pigment: rose / yellow / blue\n4           water brush (lifts pigment)\nR           reset hero canvas\nP           toggle perf overlay\nH           toggle help overlay\nEsc         close help\n```\n\n**Verified in Node:**\n- All 28 animation+background combos set cleanly (no throws)\n- `inst.perf(true)` \u2192 `perfMetrics().enabled === true`, all expected numeric fields present\n- `inst.perf(false)` \u2192 toggles off correctly\n- 1000-pick distribution test confirms all 7 animations and all 4 backgrounds appear roughly uniformly\n\nThe lib parses clean (229,777 chars), wiring parses clean (10,269 chars \u2014 up from 8,130 in v0.3 due to perf overlay polling code and random-pick logic).",

      "file_text": "# Build your own Multi-Watercolor\n\nA friendly quickstart for putting four watercolor canvases on a single page \u2014 one full-viewport hero you can paint on, plus three thumbnail cards that animate themselves on load and wash away when clicked.\n\nThis is what `multi-watercolor-v0.4.html` does. Here's the path that got us there, simplified into something you can build in an afternoon.\n\n---\n\n## What you'll need\n\n- `watercolor-lib.js` \u2014 the simulation library. One file, no dependencies, exposes a single global `Watercolor.create()` factory.\n- An HTML file. That's it. No bundler, no build step, no npm. Open it in a browser and it works.\n\nIf you have those two things, you're set.\n\n---\n\n## The big idea\n\nThe library is built around a factory function. You give it a host element and an options object; you get back an \"instance\" handle with all the painting methods. State (pigment arrays, paint loads, animation modes, the lot) is closed over per instance \u2014 they don't share anything except the global `Watercolor` namespace.\n\nThat means you can call `Watercolor.create()` as many times as you want on the same page, and each canvas gets its own world. That's the trick that makes a \"multi-watercolor\" page even possible.\n\n```js\nconst hero = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('hero'));\nconst thumb1 = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('thumb-1'));\nconst thumb2 = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('thumb-2'));\nconst thumb3 = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('thumb-3'));\n// Four independent painting systems, ~150 lines of total code between them.\n```\n\nEach instance owns its own RAF loop, its own simulation grid, its own brush state. Tell `hero` to use a big rose brush and `thumb1` to do sketch mode in blue, and they don't fight.\n\n---\n\n## The bones\n\nYou need a host div for each canvas. The lib attaches a `<canvas>` element inside whatever you give it.\n\n```html\n<!-- Full-viewport background -->\n<div class=\"hero\" id=\"hero\"></div>\n\n<!-- Three thumbnail cards over the hero -->\n<div class=\"thumbnails\">\n  <article class=\"thumb\" data-project=\"orchard\">\n    <div class=\"thumb-canvas\" id=\"thumb-1\"></div>\n    <div class=\"thumb-title\">Orchard</div>\n  </article>\n  <article class=\"thumb\" data-project=\"harbor\">\n    <div class=\"thumb-canvas\" id=\"thumb-2\"></div>\n    <div class=\"thumb-title\">Harbor</div>\n  </article>\n  <article class=\"thumb\" data-project=\"meadow\">\n    <div class=\"thumb-canvas\" id=\"thumb-3\"></div>\n    <div class=\"thumb-title\">Meadow</div>\n  </article>\n</div>\n```\n\nThe hero is the background \u2014 `position: fixed; inset: 0`. The thumbnails sit on top in their own container with a higher `z-index`. That stacking order is doing real work for you: when the user clicks a thumbnail, the DOM event system routes the click to the card (because it's on top). When they click empty hero area, the event goes to the hero canvas (because nothing else is in the way). No event-coordination code needed.\n\n---\n\n## Layout that flips for mobile\n\nThe original spec was *3 rows \u00d7 1 column on desktop, 1 row \u00d7 3 columns on mobile*. That's a one-line flex-direction flip.\n\n```css\n.thumbnails {\n  position: fixed;\n  z-index: 5;\n  top: 50%; left: 50%;\n  transform: translate(-50%, -50%);\n  display: flex;\n  flex-direction: column;       /* vertical column on desktop */\n  gap: 18px;\n  width: min(440px, calc(100vw - 48px));\n  pointer-events: none;         /* default; children re-enable */\n}\n\n.thumb {\n  width: 100%;\n  aspect-ratio: 1;              /* keep cards square */\n  pointer-events: auto;         /* re-enable clicks on the cards */\n  /* \u2026visual styling\u2026 */\n}\n\n@media (max-width: 760px) {\n  .thumbnails {\n    flex-direction: row;        /* horizontal row on mobile */\n    top: auto; left: 0; right: 0;\n    bottom: 16px;\n    transform: none;\n    width: auto;\n    padding: 0 12px;\n  }\n  .thumb {\n    flex: 1;                    /* split row evenly */\n  }\n}\n```\n\nA few details worth pointing out, because they're easy to skip and painful to debug:\n\n**`pointer-events: none` on the container, `pointer-events: auto` on the cards.** Without this, the container would catch clicks in the gaps between cards, intercept them, and the hero canvas wouldn't receive paint events when the user dragged through that area. The \"none on the parent, auto on the children\" pattern is the right hammer for \"this element is for layout only, let interactions fall through.\"\n\n**`aspect-ratio: 1`** keeps the cards square at every container size. The SVG sketches are designed for square viewBoxes, so this matters for the artwork to fit.\n\n**`flex: 1`** on mobile cards lets them split the row evenly. If the viewport is 375px wide with 12px padding and 10px gaps, each card gets `(375 - 24 - 20) / 3 = ~110px`. They stay square because of the aspect-ratio rule.\n\n---\n\n## Don't forget `touch-action: none`\n\nIf you skip this, painting on a touch device will silently scroll the page instead of leaving paint. Add it to `html, body`:\n\n```css\nhtml, body {\n  touch-action: none;\n  overflow: hidden;\n  overscroll-behavior: none;\n  -webkit-user-select: none;\n  user-select: none;\n}\n```\n\nThis tells the browser \"give every touch gesture to JS, don't consume them yourself for scrolling/zoom.\" On a drawing surface, that's what you want.\n\n---\n\n## Boot the hero\n\nThe hero is full-viewport, paintable, and runs a randomized scene on load.\n\n```js\nconst hero = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('hero'), {\n  canvasScale: 0.6,             // smaller simulation grid \u2192 smoother on modest hardware\n  pointer: true,                // enable mouse/touch/pen \u2192 paint\n  cursorPreview: false,         // use the CSS crosshair instead of a DOM cursor\n});\n\n// Brush state tuned for casual finger/mouse painting on a large surface\nhero.brushSize(60);\nhero.paintLoad(0.85);\nhero.waterLoad(1.1);\nhero.pigment('rose');\n```\n\nA few of these are worth a second of context:\n\n**`canvasScale: 0.6`** \u2014 the simulation grid is 60% of the host's pixel resolution. CSS scales the rendered output back up. You lose a little crispness; you gain a *lot* of CPU headroom. On a 1920\u00d71080 viewport, that's ~700k cells instead of ~2M. Pick lower if the hero is animation-heavy.\n\n**`pointer: true`** \u2014 this is the one option that makes the hero drawable. The lib attaches its own pointermove/pointerdown/pointerup listeners to the canvas. Set to `false` and the canvas becomes decorative (which is what you want for the thumbnails).\n\n**`cursorPreview: false`** \u2014 the lib has a flag for \"should there be a brush-shaped cursor following the pointer?\" but the actual DOM cursor element is the host's responsibility (in the main demo app it's wired up; here we just use a CSS crosshair to keep the multi-watercolor file simple).\n\n---\n\n## Random scene on load\n\nEach visit picks one animation and one background. Curate the pools to keep the first impression friendly \u2014 leave the dramatic stuff (thunderstorm, tornado) for explicit opt-in elsewhere.\n\n```js\nconst ANIMATIONS  = ['rainy', 'sunny', 'windy', 'partlyCloudy',\n                     'snowing', 'snowingAdditive', 'ai'];\nconst BACKGROUNDS = ['day', 'dawn', 'sunset', 'night'];\nconst pick = (arr) => arr[Math.floor(Math.random() * arr.length)];\n\nhero.setBackground(pick(BACKGROUNDS));   // base wash first\nhero.setAnimation(pick(ANIMATIONS));     // animation on top\n```\n\n**Order matters here.** `setBackground` is a one-shot paint of a time-of-day wash. `setAnimation` registers a continuous per-frame step function. If you set the animation first, it paints briefly, then gets covered by the background. Background first \u2192 animation second \u2192 both stay visible.\n\nThat gives 28 unique combinations (7 \u00d7 4). The page feels different every time someone lands.\n\n---\n\n## The thumbnails\n\nSame factory, different config. Each one runs `sketchMode()` (which is a settings bundle \u2014 small brush, dry paper, no bleed, no edge darkening), then traces an SVG.\n\n```js\nconst SVGS = {\n  orchard: '<svg viewBox=\"0 0 100 100\">...</svg>',\n  harbor:  '<svg viewBox=\"0 0 100 100\">...</svg>',\n  meadow:  '<svg viewBox=\"0 0 100 100\">...</svg>',\n};\n\nconst thumbConfigs = [\n  { hostId: 'thumb-1', project: 'orchard', pigment: 'blue'   },\n  { hostId: 'thumb-2', project: 'harbor',  pigment: 'rose'   },\n  { hostId: 'thumb-3', project: 'meadow',  pigment: 'yellow' },\n];\n\nconst thumbs = thumbConfigs.map((cfg, i) => {\n  const wc = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById(cfg.hostId), {\n    canvasScale: 0.7,\n    pointer: false,          // clicks go to the card, not the canvas\n    cursorPreview: false,\n  });\n  wc.sketchMode();           // felt-pen bundle\n\n  // Stagger so all three don't draw simultaneously \u2014 looks more alive\n  setTimeout(() => {\n    wc.traceSVG(SVGS[cfg.project], {\n      pigment: cfg.pigment,\n      durationMs: 1500,        // 1.5s animation\n      easing: 'penStroke',     // mimics how a hand draws\n      perStrokePauseMs: 200,   // brief pause between SVG paths\n    });\n  }, 250 + i * 350);\n\n  return { ...cfg, wc };\n});\n```\n\nA few things worth chewing on:\n\n**`canvasScale: 0.7`** on the thumbnails. They're small (~140px on desktop, ~110px on mobile) so we can afford a slightly higher resolution than the hero without trouble.\n\n**`pointer: false`** keeps the lib from listening for pointer events on the thumbnail canvas \u2014 we want clicks to route to the parent `<article>` card so we can run our own obliterate logic.\n\n**`sketchMode()`** is a one-shot settings bundle, not a mode flag. It calls a series of setters: `brushSize(7)`, `paintLoad(1.6)`, `waterLoad(0.25)`, `pressure(0.95)`, `paperWetness('boneDry')`, `continuousFlow(false)`, `edgeDarkening(false)`, `fadePainting(false)`. After it runs there's no \"sketch mode\" to turn off \u2014 just a brush configured for fine-line work.\n\n**`easing: 'penStroke'`** is the easing curve that mimics hand-drawn motion: a quick 15% ramp-up to get up to speed, a steady 70% middle, then a 15% deceleration to land. Combined with `perStrokePauseMs: 200`, you get a believable drawing rhythm \u2014 smooth strokes punctuated by pen-lift moments between SVG path elements. This is the single best feel-improvement you can make over linear timing.\n\n**The setTimeout stagger** (`250 + i * 350`) means the three thumbnails start drawing 250ms / 600ms / 950ms after load. Simultaneous animations on a page read as \"all running automatically.\" Staggered ones read as \"drawing themselves, one after another.\" Small thing, big difference.\n\n---\n\n## The click handler (where I goofed, so you don't have to)\n\nWhen you click a thumbnail, you want the wash to start *where you clicked*, not at the center. The library has `toGrid()` to convert client coordinates into the simulation's internal grid coordinates.\n\nHere's the code:\n\n```js\nthumbs.forEach((thumb) => {\n  const card = document.querySelector(`[data-project=\"${thumb.project}\"]`);\n  let busy = false;\n\n  card.addEventListener('click', async (e) => {\n    if (busy) return;\n    busy = true;\n\n    // \u26a0\ufe0f Pass clientX/clientY DIRECTLY. Don't pre-subtract rect.left.\n    // The lib does its own getBoundingClientRect() inside toGrid.\n    const { x, y } = thumb.wc.toGrid(e.clientX, e.clientY);\n\n    await thumb.wc.obliterate({\n      mode: 'water',           // water rinse \u2014 pigment lifts and diffuses\n      durationMs: 500,\n      x, y,                    // splash centered where you clicked\n    });\n\n    console.log('Project clicked:', thumb.project);\n    // In a real portfolio, navigate here: router.push(...)\n\n    // For this demo, redraw the sketch so the page stays interactive\n    setTimeout(() => {\n      thumb.wc.reset();\n      thumb.wc.sketchMode();\n      thumb.wc.traceSVG(SVGS[thumb.project], {\n        pigment: thumb.pigment, durationMs: 1200,\n        easing: 'penStroke', perStrokePauseMs: 150,\n      });\n      busy = false;\n    }, 900);\n  });\n});\n```\n\n**The gotcha I want you to skip.** Earlier versions of this demo had:\n\n```js\n// \ud83d\udea8 Wrong\nconst rect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\nconst grid = thumb.wc.toGrid(e.clientX - rect.left, e.clientY - rect.top);\nawait thumb.wc.obliterate({ x: grid.gx, y: grid.gy });\n```\n\nTwo bugs in two lines. (1) `toGrid` already does `getBoundingClientRect()` on its own canvas \u2014 pre-subtracting double-subtracts. (2) The returned object is `{x, y}`, not `{gx, gy}`. The destructured `gx`/`gy` were `undefined`, which obliterate silently defaulted to canvas center.\n\nResult: every click obliterated at center. It *looked* like it worked, because there was always some animation. But the wash never followed the click point.\n\nMoral: read the return shape, and trust the helper to do what it says. `toGrid(clientX, clientY)` is the whole interface.\n\n**The `busy` flag** prevents double-clicks while an obliterate is in flight. Without it, rapidly clicking would queue overlapping obliterates and the redraw setTimeout would fire while a previous one was still running, getting into weird states.\n\n---\n\n## Keyboard shortcuts (the only \"UI\")\n\nThe demo deliberately has no buttons. Brush selection, reset, perf, help \u2014 all keyboard.\n\n```js\ndocument.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {\n  // Skip if user is typing in a form field somewhere\n  if (e.target?.tagName === 'INPUT' || e.target?.tagName === 'TEXTAREA') return;\n\n  switch (e.key) {\n    case '1': hero.pigment('rose');   break;\n    case '2': hero.pigment('yellow'); break;\n    case '3': hero.pigment('blue');   break;\n    case '4': hero.pigment('water');  break;\n    case 'r': case 'R': hero.reset(); break;\n    case 'p': case 'P': togglePerf(); break;\n    case 'h': case 'H': toggleHelp(); break;\n    case 'Escape': closeOverlaysIfOpen();\n  }\n});\n```\n\nThe reason to skip buttons isn't dogmatic minimalism \u2014 it's that for a portfolio background, every visible control fights with the artwork for attention. Keyboard shortcuts are discoverable through a small `H`-toggled help overlay, and you can document them in a hint that fades after first paint. The result is a page that *looks* like nothing but a painted background and three cards, but rewards keyboard exploration.\n\n---\n\n## Perf overlay\n\nThe library exposes `perf(true)` to enable instrumentation and `perfMetrics()` to read the numbers. Build a tiny DOM panel and poll at 4 Hz:\n\n```js\nconst perfEl = document.getElementById('perf');\nlet perfTimer = null;\n\nfunction updatePerf() {\n  const m = hero.perfMetrics();\n  setText('perf-fps',    m.fps?.toFixed(1) ?? '\u2014');\n  setText('perf-p50',    m.framep50.toFixed(2) + 'ms');\n  setText('perf-active', m.activePct.toFixed(1) + '%');\n  // \u2026etc, see v0.4 source for the full list\n}\n\nfunction togglePerf() {\n  const open = perfEl.classList.toggle('open');\n  hero.perf(open);                 // enable/disable instrumentation\n  if (open) {\n    updatePerf();\n    perfTimer = setInterval(updatePerf, 250);\n  } else {\n    clearInterval(perfTimer);\n    perfTimer = null;\n  }\n}\n```\n\nOne subtle thing: the lib gates `performance.now()` calls behind `perfEnabled` to avoid paying for instrumentation when it's off. That means toggling perf actually changes what you're measuring slightly \u2014 a kind of perf Heisenberg. For a portfolio page this doesn't matter; for serious profiling, leave it on for the whole session.\n\n---\n\n## Going further\n\nIdeas that fit naturally:\n\n- **Project navigation on click**: replace the demo's redraw with an actual route push. `router.push('/projects/' + thumb.project)` after the obliterate completes.\n- **Raster-trace thumbnails**: instead of SVG paths, sample pixels from a PNG and `paintAt` each one. The obliterate animation works unchanged \u2014 it doesn't care how the pigment got onto the canvas.\n- **Persistent painting**: serialize the hero's grid arrays to localStorage on `beforeunload`, restore on boot. The user's doodles survive across visits.\n- **Click-and-hold for sustained pour**: increase the brush's `flow()` value while a pointer is held down, return to default on release. Felt-tip \u2192 fountain pen \u2192 faucet.\n- **Color from URL**: parse `?pigment=blue` from `location.search` and apply on load. Lets you link to specific moods.\n\nThe library is the same in all these cases. You're just composing its primitives \u2014 `paintAt`, `traceSVG`, `obliterate`, `setAnimation`, `setBackground`, the brush-state setters \u2014 in different patterns.\n\n---\n\n## A short list of things to remember\n\n1. **One instance per host element.** Each `Watercolor.create()` call is independent. State doesn't bleed.\n2. **`pointer: true` for drawable surfaces, `false` for displays.** This is the single most important option.\n3. **Z-order routes clicks naturally.** Cards above hero in stacking order = clicks on cards go to cards, clicks elsewhere go to hero. No coordination code needed.\n4. **`toGrid(clientX, clientY)` returns `{x, y}`.** Pass raw client coordinates. Read raw `x` and `y`. Anything else is a bug.\n5. **`touch-action: none` on the html/body** or finger-painting will scroll the page instead of leaving paint.\n6. **Background before animation.** Setting an animation first then a background covers the animation with the wash.\n\nHave fun. The library is built for this kind of composition \u2014 you're not fighting it, you're using it the way it wants to be used. Make something weird.\n",

CSS scales the rendered output back up. You lose a little crispness; you gain a *lot* of CPU headroom. On a 1920\\u00d71080 viewport, that's ~700k cells instead of ~2M. Pick lower if the hero is animation-heavy.\\n\\n**`pointer: true`** \\u2014 this is the one option that makes the hero drawable. The lib attaches its own pointermove/pointerdown/pointerup listeners to the canvas. Set to `false` and the canvas becomes decorative (which is what you want for the thumbnails).\\n\\n**`cursorPreview: false`** \\u2014 the lib has a flag for \\\"should there be a brush-shaped cursor following the pointer?\\\" but the actual DOM cursor element is the host's responsibility (in the main demo app it's wired up; here we just use a CSS crosshair to keep the multi-watercolor file simple).\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Random scene on load\\n\\nEach visit picks one animation and one background. Curate the pools to keep the first impression friendly \\u2014 leave the dramatic stuff (thunderstorm, tornado) for explicit opt-in elsewhere.\\n\\n```js\\nconst ANIMATIONS  = ['rainy', 'sunny', 'windy', 'partlyCloudy',\\n                     'snowing', 'snowingAdditive', 'ai'];\\nconst BACKGROUNDS = ['day', 'dawn', 'sunset', 'night'];\\nconst pick = (arr) => arr[Math.floor(Math.random() * arr.length)];\\n\\nhero.setBackground(pick(BACKGROUNDS));   // base wash first\\nhero.setAnimation(pick(ANIMATIONS));     // animation on top\\n```\\n\\n**Order matters here.** `setBackground` is a one-shot paint of a time-of-day wash. `setAnimation` registers a continuous per-frame step function. If you set the animation first, it paints briefly, then gets covered by the background. Background first \\u2192 animation second \\u2192 both stay visible.\\n\\nThat gives 28 unique combinations (7 \\u00d7 4). The page feels different every time someone lands.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## The thumbnails\\n\\nSame factory, different config. Each one runs `sketchMode()` (which is a settings bundle \\u2014 small brush, dry paper, no bleed, no edge darkening), then traces an SVG.\\n\\n```js\\nconst SVGS = {\\n  orchard: '<svg viewBox=\\\"0 0 100 100\\\">...</svg>',\\n  harbor:  '<svg viewBox=\\\"0 0 100 100\\\">...</svg>',\\n  meadow:  '<svg viewBox=\\\"0 0 100 100\\\">...</svg>',\\n};\\n\\nconst thumbConfigs = [\\n  { hostId: 'thumb-1', project: 'orchard', pigment: 'blue'   },\\n  { hostId: 'thumb-2', project: 'harbor',  pigment: 'rose'   },\\n  { hostId: 'thumb-3', project: 'meadow',  pigment: 'yellow' },\\n];\\n\\nconst thumbs = thumbConfigs.map((cfg, i) => {\\n  const wc = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById(cfg.hostId), {\\n    canvasScale: 0.7,\\n    pointer: false,          // clicks go to the card, not the canvas\\n    cursorPreview: false,\\n  });\\n  wc.sketchMode();           // felt-pen bundle\\n\\n  // Stagger so all three don't draw simultaneously \\u2014 looks more alive\\n  setTimeout(() => {\\n    wc.traceSVG(SVGS[cfg.project], {\\n      pigment: cfg.pigment,\\n      durationMs: 1500,        // 1.5s animation\\n      easing: 'penStroke',     // mimics how a hand draws\\n      perStrokePauseMs: 200,   // brief pause between SVG paths\\n    });\\n  }, 250 + i * 350);\\n\\n  return { ...cfg, wc };\\n});\\n```\\n\\nA few things worth chewing on:\\n\\n**`canvasScale: 0.7`** on the thumbnails. They're small (~140px on desktop, ~110px on mobile) so we can afford a slightly higher resolution than the hero without trouble.\\n\\n**`pointer: false`** keeps the lib from listening for pointer events on the thumbnail canvas \\u2014 we want clicks to route to the parent `<article>` card so we can run our own obliterate logic.\\n\\n**`sketchMode()`** is a one-shot settings bundle, not a mode flag. It calls a series of setters: `brushSize(7)`, `paintLoad(1.6)`, `waterLoad(0.25)`, `pressure(0.95)`, `paperWetness('boneDry')`, `continuousFlow(false)`, `edgeDarkening(false)`, `fadePainting(false)`. After it runs there's no \\\"sketch mode\\\" to turn off \\u2014 just a brush configured for fine-line work.\\n\\n**`easing: 'penStroke'`** is the easing curve that mimics hand-drawn motion: a quick 15% ramp-up to get up to speed, a steady 70% middle, then a 15% deceleration to land. Combined with `perStrokePauseMs: 200`, you get a believable drawing rhythm \\u2014 smooth strokes punctuated by pen-lift moments between SVG path elements. This is the single best feel-improvement you can make over linear timing.\\n\\n**The setTimeout stagger** (`250 + i * 350`) means the three thumbnails start drawing 250ms / 600ms / 950ms after load. Simultaneous animations on a page read as \\\"all running automatically.\\\" Staggered ones read as \\\"drawing themselves, one after another.\\\" Small thing, big difference.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## The click handler (where I goofed, so you don't have to)\\n\\nWhen you click a thumbnail, you want the wash to start *where you clicked*, not at the center. The library has `toGrid()` to convert client coordinates into the simulation's internal grid coordinates.\\n\\nHere's the code:\\n\\n```js\\nthumbs.forEach((thumb) => {\\n  const card = document.querySelector(`[data-project=\\\"${thumb.project}\\\"]`);\\n  let busy = false;\\n\\n  card.addEventListener('click', async (e) => {\\n    if (busy) return;\\n    busy = true;\\n\\n    // \\u26a0\\ufe0f Pass clientX/clientY DIRECTLY. Don't pre-subtract rect.left.\\n    // The lib does its own getBoundingClientRect() inside toGrid.\\n    const { x, y } = thumb.wc.toGrid(e.clientX, e.clientY);\\n\\n    await thumb.wc.obliterate({\\n      mode: 'water',           // water rinse \\u2014 pigment lifts and diffuses\\n      durationMs: 500,\\n      x, y,                    // splash centered where you clicked\\n    });\\n\\n    console.log('Project clicked:', thumb.project);\\n    // In a real portfolio, navigate here: router.push(...)\\n\\n    // For this demo, redraw the sketch so the page stays interactive\\n    setTimeout(() => {\\n      thumb.wc.reset();\\n      thumb.wc.sketchMode();\\n      thumb.wc.traceSVG(SVGS[thumb.project], {\\n        pigment: thumb.pigment, durationMs: 1200,\\n        easing: 'penStroke', perStrokePauseMs: 150,\\n      });\\n      busy = false;\\n    }, 900);\\n  });\\n});\\n```\\n\\n**The gotcha I want you to skip.** Earlier versions of this demo had:\\n\\n```js\\n// \\ud83d\\udea8 Wrong\\nconst rect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\\nconst grid = thumb.wc.toGrid(e.clientX - rect.left, e.clientY - rect.top);\\nawait thumb.wc.obliterate({ x: grid.gx, y: grid.gy });\\n```\\n\\nTwo bugs in two lines. (1) `toGrid` already does `getBoundingClientRect()` on its own canvas \\u2014 pre-subtracting double-subtracts. (2) The returned object is `{x, y}`, not `{gx, gy}`. The destructured `gx`/`gy` were `undefined`, which obliterate silently defaulted to canvas center.\\n\\nResult: every click obliterated at center. It *looked* like it worked, because there was always some animation. But the wash never followed the click point.\\n\\nMoral: read the return shape, and trust the helper to do what it says. `toGrid(clientX, clientY)` is the whole interface.\\n\\n**The `busy` flag** prevents double-clicks while an obliterate is in flight. Without it, rapidly clicking would queue overlapping obliterates and the redraw setTimeout would fire while a previous one was still running, getting into weird states.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Keyboard shortcuts (the only \\\"UI\\\")\\n\\nThe demo deliberately has no buttons. Brush selection, reset, perf, help \\u2014 all keyboard.\\n\\n```js\\ndocument.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {\\n  // Skip if user is typing in a form field somewhere\\n  if (e.target?.tagName === 'INPUT' || e.target?.tagName === 'TEXTAREA') return;\\n\\n  switch (e.key) {\\n    case '1': hero.pigment('rose');   break;\\n    case '2': hero.pigment('yellow'); break;\\n    case '3': hero.pigment('blue');   break;\\n    case '4': hero.pigment('water');  break;\\n    case 'r': case 'R': hero.reset(); break;\\n    case 'p': case 'P': togglePerf(); break;\\n    case 'h': case 'H': toggleHelp(); break;\\n    case 'Escape': closeOverlaysIfOpen();\\n  }\\n});\\n```\\n\\nThe reason to skip buttons isn't dogmatic minimalism \\u2014 it's that for a portfolio background, every visible control fights with the artwork for attention. Keyboard shortcuts are discoverable through a small `H`-toggled help overlay, and you can document them in a hint that fades after first paint. The result is a page that *looks* like nothing but a painted background and three cards, but rewards keyboard exploration.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Perf overlay\\n\\nThe library exposes `perf(true)` to enable instrumentation and `perfMetrics()` to read the numbers. Build a tiny DOM panel and poll at 4 Hz:\\n\\n```js\\nconst perfEl = document.getElementById('perf');\\nlet perfTimer = null;\\n\\nfunction updatePerf() {\\n  const m = hero.perfMetrics();\\n  setText('perf-fps',    m.fps?.toFixed(1) ?? '\\u2014');\\n  setText('perf-p50',    m.framep50.toFixed(2) + 'ms');\\n  setText('perf-active', m.activePct.toFixed(1) + '%');\\n  // \\u2026etc, see v0.4 source for the full list\\n}\\n\\nfunction togglePerf() {\\n  const open = perfEl.classList.toggle('open');\\n  hero.perf(open);                 // enable/disable instrumentation\\n  if (open) {\\n    updatePerf();\\n    perfTimer = setInterval(updatePerf, 250);\\n  } else {\\n    clearInterval(perfTimer);\\n    perfTimer = null;\\n  }\\n}\\n```\\n\\nOne subtle thing: the lib gates `performance.now()` calls behind `perfEnabled` to avoid paying for instrumentation when it's off. That means toggling perf actually changes what you're measuring slightly \\u2014 a kind of perf Heisenberg. For a portfolio page this doesn't matter; for serious profiling, leave it on for the whole session.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Going further\\n\\nIdeas that fit naturally:\\n\\n- **Project navigation on click**: replace the demo's redraw with an actual route push. `router.push('/projects/' + thumb.project)` after the obliterate completes.\\n- **Raster-trace thumbnails**: instead of SVG paths, sample pixels from a PNG and `paintAt` each one. The obliterate animation works unchanged \\u2014 it doesn't care how the pigment got onto the canvas.\\n- **Persistent painting**: serialize the hero's grid arrays to localStorage on `beforeunload`, restore on boot. The user's doodles survive across visits.\\n- **Click-and-hold for sustained pour**: increase the brush's `flow()` value while a pointer is held down, return to default on release. Felt-tip \\u2192 fountain pen \\u2192 faucet.\\n- **Color from URL**: parse `?pigment=blue` from `location.search` and apply on load. Lets you link to specific moods.\\n\\nThe library is the same in all these cases. You're just composing its primitives \\u2014 `paintAt`, `traceSVG`, `obliterate`, `setAnimation`, `setBackground`, the brush-state setters \\u2014 in different patterns.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## A short list of things to remember\\n\\n1. **One instance per host element.** Each `Watercolor.create()` call is independent. State doesn't bleed.\\n2. **`pointer: true` for drawable surfaces, `false` for displays.** This is the single most important option.\\n3. **Z-order routes clicks naturally.** Cards above hero in stacking order = clicks on cards go to cards, clicks elsewhere go to hero. No coordination code needed.\\n4. **`toGrid(clientX, clientY)` returns `{x, y}`.** Pass raw client coordinates. Read raw `x` and `y`. Anything else is a bug.\\n5. **`touch-action: none` on the html/body** or finger-painting will scroll the page instead of leaving paint.\\n6. **Background before animation.** Setting an animation first then a background covers the animation with the wash.\\n\\nHave fun. The library is built for this kind of composition \\u2014 you're not fighting it, you're using it the way it wants to be used. Make something weird.\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/multi-watercolor-quickstart.md\"}"

The host's only job is to call methods when\n  2127\t          state changes are wanted.\n  2128\t        </p>\n  2129\t        <p>\n  2130\t          <strong>Coordinate system.</strong> All position arguments\n  2131\t          (<code>paintAt</code>, <code>paintText</code>, cursor\n  2132\t          positions) are in <em>grid coordinates</em>: integer cells\n  2133\t          on the simulation grid, not display pixels. The library implements the Curtis et al.\\n          (1997) watercolor model with Kubelka&ndash;Munk compositing,\\n          shallow-water flow on a staggered grid (Foster &amp;\\n          Metaxas 1996), and edge darkening. Runs CPU-side by default\\n          with an optional WebGL2 render path for compositing.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>How to mount.</strong> The library exposes a\\n          factory:\\n        </p>\\n        <pre class=\\\"docs-code\\\">const wc = Watercolor.create(targetEl, options?);</pre>\\n        <p>\\n          Every method on the returned <code>wc</code> handle is\\n          stateful and synchronous. There are no callbacks, no\\n          Promises (except <code>traceSVG</code> when passed a URL),\\n          and no event-loop coordination required from the caller.\\n          The simulation runs on a single\\n          <code>requestAnimationFrame</code> loop owned by the\\n          instance. The host's only job is to call methods when\\n          state changes are wanted.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Coordinate system.</strong> All position arguments\\n          (<code>paintAt</code>, <code>paintText</code>, cursor\\n          positions) are in <em>grid coordinates</em>: integer cells\\n          on the simulation grid, not display pixels. Read\\n          <code>wc.grid.width</code> /\\n          <code>wc.grid.height</code> for current dimensions. Convert\\n          display-pixel to grid via <code>wc.toGrid(clientX,\\n          clientY)</code>.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          <strong>Idle behavior.</strong> The sim auto-idles when no\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/watercolor.html\"}"
