      "description": "Insert Multi-Watercolor docs section",
      "new_str": "      <!-- ============================== MULTI-WATERCOLOR ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"multi\">\n        <h1 id=\"multi-intro\">Multi-Watercolor quickstart</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          A friendly walkthrough for putting four watercolor canvases\n          on a single page &mdash; one full-viewport hero you can\n          paint on, plus three thumbnail cards that animate themselves\n          on load and wash away when clicked. This is what the\n          <code>multi-watercolor-v0.4.html</code> demo does; here is\n          the path that got us there, simplified into something\n          you can build in an afternoon.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"multi-needs\">What you'll need</h2>\n        <p>\n          Two things: <code>watercolor-lib.js</code> (the simulation\n          library &mdash; one file, no dependencies, exposes a single\n          global <code>Watercolor.create()</code> factory) and an HTML\n          file. No bundler, no build step, no npm. Open it in a\n          browser and it works.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"multi-big-idea\">The big idea</h2>\n        <p>\n          The library is built around a factory function. You give it\n          a host element and an options object; you get back an\n          \"instance\" handle with all the painting methods. State\n          (pigment arrays, paint loads, animation modes, the lot) is\n          closed over per instance &mdash; they don't share anything\n          except the global <code>Watercolor</code> namespace.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          That means you can call <code>Watercolor.create()</code> as\n          many times as you want on the same page, and each canvas\n          gets its own world. That's the trick that makes a\n          \"multi-watercolor\" page possible at all.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const hero   = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('hero'));\nconst thumb1 = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('thumb-1'));\nconst thumb2 = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('thumb-2'));\nconst thumb3 = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('thumb-3'));\n// Four independent painting systems, ~150 lines of total code between them.</pre>\n        <p>\n          Each instance owns its own RAF loop, its own simulation\n          grid, its own brush state. Tell <code>hero</code> to use a\n          big rose brush and <code>thumb1</code> to do sketch mode in\n          blue, and they don't fight.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"multi-bones\">The bones</h2>\n        <p>\n          You need a host div for each canvas. The lib attaches a\n          <code>&lt;canvas&gt;</code> element inside whatever you\n          give it.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">&lt;!-- Full-viewport background --&gt;\n&lt;div class=\"hero\" id=\"hero\"&gt;&lt;/div&gt;\n\n&lt;!-- Three thumbnail cards over the hero --&gt;\n&lt;div class=\"thumbnails\"&gt;\n  &lt;article class=\"thumb\" data-project=\"orchard\"&gt;\n    &lt;div class=\"thumb-canvas\" id=\"thumb-1\"&gt;&lt;/div&gt;\n    &lt;div class=\"thumb-title\"&gt;Orchard&lt;/div&gt;\n  &lt;/article&gt;\n  &lt;article class=\"thumb\" data-project=\"harbor\"&gt;\n    &lt;div class=\"thumb-canvas\" id=\"thumb-2\"&gt;&lt;/div&gt;\n    &lt;div class=\"thumb-title\"&gt;Harbor&lt;/div&gt;\n  &lt;/article&gt;\n  &lt;article class=\"thumb\" data-project=\"meadow\"&gt;\n    &lt;div class=\"thumb-canvas\" id=\"thumb-3\"&gt;&lt;/div&gt;\n    &lt;div class=\"thumb-title\"&gt;Meadow&lt;/div&gt;\n  &lt;/article&gt;\n&lt;/div&gt;</pre>\n        <p>\n          The hero is the background &mdash;\n          <code>position: fixed; inset: 0</code>. The thumbnails sit\n          on top in their own container with a higher\n          <code>z-index</code>. That stacking order is doing real\n          work for you: when the user clicks a thumbnail, the DOM\n          event system routes the click to the card (because it's on\n          top). When they click empty hero area, the event goes to\n          the hero canvas (because nothing else is in the way). No\n          event-coordination code needed.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"multi-layout\">Layout that flips for mobile</h2>\n        <p>\n          The original spec was <em>3 rows &times; 1 column on\n          desktop, 1 row &times; 3 columns on mobile</em>. That's a\n          one-line flex-direction flip.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">.thumbnails {\n  position: fixed;\n  z-index: 5;\n  top: 50%; left: 50%;\n  transform: translate(-50%, -50%);\n  display: flex;\n  flex-direction: column;       /* vertical column on desktop */\n  gap: 18px;\n  width: min(440px, calc(100vw - 48px));\n  pointer-events: none;         /* default; children re-enable */\n}\n\n.thumb {\n  width: 100%;\n  aspect-ratio: 1;              /* keep cards square */\n  pointer-events: auto;         /* re-enable clicks on the cards */\n  /* \u2026visual styling\u2026 */\n}\n\n@media (max-width: 760px) {\n  .thumbnails {\n    flex-direction: row;        /* horizontal row on mobile */\n    top: auto; left: 0; right: 0;\n    bottom: 16px;\n    transform: none;\n    width: auto;\n    padding: 0 12px;\n  }\n  .thumb {\n    flex: 1;                    /* split row evenly */\n  }\n}</pre>\n        <p>\n          A few details worth pointing out, because they're easy to\n          skip and painful to debug:\n        </p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt><code>pointer-events: none</code> on the container,\n            <code>pointer-events: auto</code> on the cards</dt>\n          <dd>Without this, the container would catch clicks in the gaps between cards, intercept them, and the hero canvas wouldn't receive paint events when the user dragged through that area. The \"none on the parent, auto on the children\" pattern is the right hammer for \"this element is for layout only, let interactions fall through.\"</dd>\n          <dt><code>aspect-ratio: 1</code></dt>\n          <dd>Keeps the cards square at every container size. The SVG sketches are designed for square viewBoxes, so this matters for the artwork to fit.</dd>\n          <dt><code>flex: 1</code> on mobile cards</dt>\n          <dd>Lets them split the row evenly. If the viewport is 375px wide with 12px padding and 10px gaps, each card gets <code>(375 - 24 - 20) / 3 = ~110px</code>. They stay square because of the aspect-ratio rule.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"multi-touch-action\">Don't forget <code>touch-action: none</code></h2>\n        <p>\n          If you skip this, painting on a touch device will silently\n          scroll the page instead of leaving paint. Add it to\n          <code>html, body</code>:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">html, body {\n  touch-action: none;\n  overflow: hidden;\n  overscroll-behavior: none;\n  -webkit-user-select: none;\n  user-select: none;\n}</pre>\n        <p>\n          This tells the browser \"give every touch gesture to JS,\n          don't consume them yourself for scrolling/zoom.\" On a\n          drawing surface, that's what you want.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"multi-hero\">Boot the hero</h2>\n        <p>\n          The hero is full-viewport, paintable, and runs a\n          randomized scene on load.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const hero = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('hero'), {\n  canvasScale: 0.6,             // smaller simulation grid \u2192 smoother on modest hardware\n  pointer: true,                // enable mouse/touch/pen \u2192 paint\n  cursorPreview: false,         // use the CSS crosshair instead of a DOM cursor\n});\n\n// Brush state tuned for casual finger/mouse painting on a large surface\nhero.brushSize(60);\nhero.paintLoad(0.85);\nhero.waterLoad(1.1);\nhero.pigment('rose');</pre>\n        <p>\n          A few of these are worth a second of context:\n        </p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt><code>canvasScale: 0.6</code></dt>\n          <dd>The simulation grid is 60% of the host's pixel resolution. CSS scales the rendered output back up. You lose a little crispness; you gain a <em>lot</em> of CPU headroom. On a 1920&times;1080 viewport, that's ~700k cells instead of ~2M. Pick lower if the hero is animation-heavy.</dd>\n          <dt><code>pointer: true</code></dt>\n          <dd>This is the one option that makes the hero drawable. The lib attaches its own pointermove/pointerdown/pointerup listeners to the canvas. Set to <code>false</code> and the canvas becomes decorative (which is what you want for the thumbnails).</dd>\n          <dt><code>cursorPreview: false</code></dt>\n          <dd>The lib has a flag for \"should there be a brush-shaped cursor following the pointer?\" but the actual DOM cursor element is the host's responsibility (in the main demo app it's wired up; here we just use a CSS crosshair to keep the multi-watercolor file simple).</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"multi-random\">Random scene on load</h2>\n        <p>\n          Each visit picks one animation and one background. Curate\n          the pools to keep the first impression friendly &mdash;\n          leave the dramatic stuff (thunderstorm, tornado) for\n          explicit opt-in elsewhere.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const ANIMATIONS  = ['rainy', 'sunny', 'windy', 'partlyCloudy',\n                     'snowing', 'snowingAdditive', 'ai'];\nconst BACKGROUNDS = ['day', 'dawn', 'sunset', 'night'];\nconst pick = (arr) =&gt; arr[Math.floor(Math.random() * arr.length)];\n\nhero.setBackground(pick(BACKGROUNDS));   // base wash first\nhero.setAnimation(pick(ANIMATIONS));     // animation on top</pre>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Order matters here.</strong>\n          <code>setBackground</code> is a one-shot paint of a\n          time-of-day wash. <code>setAnimation</code> registers a\n          continuous per-frame step function. If you set the\n          animation first, it paints briefly, then gets covered by\n          the background. Background first &rarr; animation second\n          &rarr; both stay visible.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          That gives 28 unique combinations (7 &times; 4). The page\n          feels different every time someone lands.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"multi-thumbs\">The thumbnails</h2>\n        <p>\n          Same factory, different config. Each one runs\n          <code>sketchMode()</code> (which is a settings bundle\n          &mdash; small brush, dry paper, no bleed, no edge darkening),\n          then traces an SVG.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const SVGS = {\n  orchard: '&lt;svg viewBox=\"0 0 100 100\"&gt;...&lt;/svg&gt;',\n  harbor:  '&lt;svg viewBox=\"0 0 100 100\"&gt;...&lt;/svg&gt;',\n  meadow:  '&lt;svg viewBox=\"0 0 100 100\"&gt;...&lt;/svg&gt;',\n};\n\nconst thumbConfigs = [\n  { hostId: 'thumb-1', project: 'orchard', pigment: 'blue'   },\n  { hostId: 'thumb-2', project: 'harbor',  pigment: 'rose'   },\n  { hostId: 'thumb-3', project: 'meadow',  pigment: 'yellow' },\n];\n\nconst thumbs = thumbConfigs.map((cfg, i) =&gt; {\n  const wc = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById(cfg.hostId), {\n    canvasScale: 0.7,\n    pointer: false,          // clicks go to the card, not the canvas\n    cursorPreview: false,\n  });\n  wc.sketchMode();           // felt-pen bundle\n\n  // Stagger so all three don't draw simultaneously \u2014 looks more alive\n  setTimeout(() =&gt; {\n    wc.traceSVG(SVGS[cfg.project], {\n      pigment: cfg.pigment,\n      durationMs: 1500,        // 1.5s animation\n      easing: 'penStroke',     // mimics how a hand draws\n      perStrokePauseMs: 200,   // brief pause between SVG paths\n    });\n  }, 250 + i * 350);\n\n  return { ...cfg, wc };\n});</pre>\n        <p>\n          A few things worth chewing on:\n        </p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt><code>canvasScale: 0.7</code> on the thumbnails</dt>\n          <dd>They're small (~140px on desktop, ~110px on mobile) so we can afford a slightly higher resolution than the hero without trouble.</dd>\n          <dt><code>pointer: false</code></dt>\n          <dd>Keeps the lib from listening for pointer events on the thumbnail canvas &mdash; we want clicks to route to the parent <code>&lt;article&gt;</code> card so we can run our own obliterate logic.</dd>\n          <dt><code>sketchMode()</code></dt>\n          <dd>A one-shot settings bundle, not a mode flag. It calls a series of setters: <code>brushSize(7)</code>, <code>paintLoad(1.6)</code>, <code>waterLoad(0.25)</code>, <code>pressure(0.95)</code>, <code>paperWetness('boneDry')</code>, <code>continuousFlow(false)</code>, <code>edgeDarkening(false)</code>, <code>fadePainting(false)</code>. After it runs there's no \"sketch mode\" to turn off &mdash; just a brush configured for fine-line work.</dd>\n          <dt><code>easing: 'penStroke'</code></dt>\n          <dd>The easing curve that mimics hand-drawn motion: a quick 15% ramp-up to get up to speed, a steady 70% middle, then a 15% deceleration to land. Combined with <code>perStrokePauseMs: 200</code>, you get a believable drawing rhythm &mdash; smooth strokes punctuated by pen-lift moments between SVG path elements. This is the single best feel-improvement you can make over linear timing.</dd>\n          <dt>The setTimeout stagger</dt>\n          <dd><code>250 + i * 350</code> means the three thumbnails start drawing 250ms / 600ms / 950ms after load. Simultaneous animations on a page read as \"all running automatically.\" Staggered ones read as \"drawing themselves, one after another.\" Small thing, big difference.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"multi-click\">The click handler (where I goofed, so you don't have to)</h2>\n        <p>\n          When you click a thumbnail, you want the wash to start\n          <em>where you clicked</em>, not at the center. The library\n          has <code>toGrid()</code> to convert client coordinates\n          into the simulation's internal grid coordinates.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">thumbs.forEach((thumb) =&gt; {\n  const card = document.querySelector(`[data-project=\"${thumb.project}\"]`);\n  let busy = false;\n\n  card.addEventListener('click', async (e) =&gt; {\n    if (busy) return;\n    busy = true;\n\n    // \u26a0\ufe0f Pass clientX/clientY DIRECTLY. Don't pre-subtract rect.left.\n    // The lib does its own getBoundingClientRect() inside toGrid.\n    const { x, y } = thumb.wc.toGrid(e.clientX, e.clientY);\n\n    await thumb.wc.obliterate({\n      mode: 'water',           // water rinse \u2014 pigment lifts and diffuses\n      durationMs: 500,\n      x, y,                    // splash centered where you clicked\n    });\n\n    console.log('Project clicked:', thumb.project);\n    // In a real portfolio, navigate here: router.push(...)\n\n    // For this demo, redraw the sketch so the page stays interactive\n    setTimeout(() =&gt; {\n      thumb.wc.reset();\n      thumb.wc.sketchMode();\n      thumb.wc.traceSVG(SVGS[thumb.project], {\n        pigment: thumb.pigment, durationMs: 1200,\n        easing: 'penStroke', perStrokePauseMs: 150,\n      });\n      busy = false;\n    }, 900);\n  });\n});</pre>\n        <p>\n          <strong>The gotcha I want you to skip.</strong> Earlier\n          versions of this demo had:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// \ud83d\udea8 Wrong\nconst rect = card.getBoundingClientRect();\nconst grid = thumb.wc.toGrid(e.clientX - rect.left, e.clientY - rect.top);\nawait thumb.wc.obliterate({ x: grid.gx, y: grid.gy });</pre>\n        <p>\n          Two bugs in two lines. (1) <code>toGrid</code> already does\n          <code>getBoundingClientRect()</code> on its own canvas\n          &mdash; pre-subtracting double-subtracts. (2) The returned\n          object is <code>{x, y}</code>, not <code>{gx, gy}</code>.\n          The destructured <code>gx</code>/<code>gy</code> were\n          <code>undefined</code>, which obliterate silently defaulted\n          to canvas center.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Result: every click obliterated at center. It <em>looked</em>\n          like it worked, because there was always some animation.\n          But the wash never followed the click point.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Moral: read the return shape, and trust the helper to do\n          what it says. <code>toGrid(clientX, clientY)</code> is the\n          whole interface.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The <code>busy</code> flag prevents double-clicks while an\n          obliterate is in flight. Without it, rapidly clicking would\n          queue overlapping obliterates and the redraw setTimeout\n          would fire while a previous one was still running, getting\n          into weird states.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"multi-keys\">Keyboard shortcuts (the only \"UI\")</h2>\n        <p>\n          The demo deliberately has no buttons. Brush selection,\n          reset, perf, help &mdash; all keyboard.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">document.addEventListener('keydown', (e) =&gt; {\n  // Skip if user is typing in a form field somewhere\n  if (e.target?.tagName === 'INPUT' || e.target?.tagName === 'TEXTAREA') return;\n\n  switch (e.key) {\n    case '1': hero.pigment('rose');   break;\n    case '2': hero.pigment('yellow'); break;\n    case '3': hero.pigment('blue');   break;\n    case '4': hero.pigment('water');  break;\n    case 'r': case 'R': hero.reset(); break;\n    case 'p': case 'P': togglePerf(); break;\n    case 'h': case 'H': toggleHelp(); break;\n    case 'Escape': closeOverlaysIfOpen();\n  }\n});</pre>\n        <p>\n          The reason to skip buttons isn't dogmatic minimalism\n          &mdash; it's that for a portfolio background, every visible\n          control fights with the artwork for attention. Keyboard\n          shortcuts are discoverable through a small\n          <code>H</code>-toggled help overlay, and you can document\n          them in a hint that fades after first paint. The result is\n          a page that <em>looks</em> like nothing but a painted\n          background and three cards, but rewards keyboard exploration.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"multi-perf\">Perf overlay</h2>\n        <p>\n          The library exposes <code>perf(true)</code> to enable\n          instrumentation and <code>perfMetrics()</code> to read the\n          numbers. Build a tiny DOM panel and poll at 4 Hz:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const perfEl = document.getElementById('perf');\nlet perfTimer = null;\n\nfunction updatePerf() {\n  const m = hero.perfMetrics();\n  setText('perf-fps',    m.fps?.toFixed(1) ?? '\u2014');\n  setText('perf-p50',    m.framep50.toFixed(2) + 'ms');\n  setText('perf-active', m.activePct.toFixed(1) + '%');\n  // \u2026etc, see v0.4 source for the full list\n}\n\nfunction togglePerf() {\n  const open = perfEl.classList.toggle('open');\n  hero.perf(open);                 // enable/disable instrumentation\n  if (open) {\n    updatePerf();\n    perfTimer = setInterval(updatePerf, 250);\n  } else {\n    clearInterval(perfTimer);\n    perfTimer = null;\n  }\n}</pre>\n        <p>\n          One subtle thing: the lib gates\n          <code>performance.now()</code> calls behind\n          <code>perfEnabled</code> to avoid paying for instrumentation\n          when it's off. That means toggling perf actually changes\n          what you're measuring slightly &mdash; a kind of perf\n          Heisenberg. For a portfolio page this doesn't matter; for\n          serious profiling, leave it on for the whole session.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"multi-going-further\">Going further</h2>\n        <p>\n          Ideas that fit naturally:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Project navigation on click</strong>: replace the demo's redraw with an actual route push. <code>router.push('/projects/' + thumb.project)</code> after the obliterate completes.</li>\n          <li><strong>Raster-trace thumbnails</strong>: instead of SVG paths, sample pixels from a PNG and <code>paintAt</code> each one. The obliterate animation works unchanged &mdash; it doesn't care how the pigment got onto the canvas.</li>\n          <li><strong>Persistent painting</strong>: serialize the hero's grid arrays to <code>localStorage</code> on <code>beforeunload</code>, restore on boot. The user's doodles survive across visits.</li>\n          <li><strong>Click-and-hold for sustained pour</strong>: increase the brush's <code>flow()</code> value while a pointer is held down, return to default on release. Felt-tip &rarr; fountain pen &rarr; faucet.</li>\n          <li><strong>Color from URL</strong>: parse <code>?pigment=blue</code> from <code>location.search</code> and apply on load. Lets you link to specific moods.</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          The library is the same in all these cases. You're just\n          composing its primitives &mdash; <code>paintAt</code>,\n          <code>traceSVG</code>, <code>obliterate</code>,\n          <code>setAnimation</code>, <code>setBackground</code>, the\n          brush-state setters &mdash; in different patterns.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"multi-remember\">A short list of things to remember</h2>\n        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>One instance per host element.</strong> Each <code>Watercolor.create()</code> call is independent. State doesn't bleed.</li>\n          <li><strong><code>pointer: true</code> for drawable surfaces, <code>false</code> for displays.</strong> This is the single most important option.</li>\n          <li><strong>Z-order routes clicks naturally.</strong> Cards above hero in stacking order = clicks on cards go to cards, clicks elsewhere go to hero. No coordination code needed.</li>\n          <li><strong><code>toGrid(clientX, clientY)</code> returns <code>{x, y}</code>.</strong> Pass raw client coordinates. Read raw <code>x</code> and <code>y</code>. Anything else is a bug.</li>\n          <li><strong><code>touch-action: none</code> on the html/body</strong> or finger-painting will scroll the page instead of leaving paint.</li>\n          <li><strong>Background before animation.</strong> Setting an animation first then a background covers the animation with the wash.</li>\n        </ol>\n        <p>\n          Have fun. The library is built for this kind of\n          composition &mdash; you're not fighting it, you're using it\n          the way it wants to be used. Make something weird.\n        </p>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== CONCEPTS ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"concepts\">",

    "type": "thinking",
    "thinking": " The user is asking about performance testing strategies for a resource-intensive watercolor simulation, especially given limited access to physical mobile devices. They're dealing with a CPU-heavy project where performance matters most on lower-powered devices, and they need practical approaches to validate that it runs well across different hardware constraints.\n\nI'm thinking through the concrete tools available in Chrome DevTools for this kind of testing. The Performance tab lets me simulate slower devices with CPU throttling at various levels, and I can use the rendering diagnostics to spot bottlenecks like long animation frames and paint times. Device emulation handles viewport and touch events, though it won't actually slow down execution. I can also leverage the browser's built-in performance APIs like `performance.now()` and `PerformanceObserver` to track metrics directly in the code, plus `performance.memory` to monitor heap usage during simulation.\n\nBeyond DevTools, there are several approaches depending on what I need. Lighthouse gives me automated audits with simulated mobile conditions, while WebPageTest and PageSpeed Insights offer more detailed analysis. For real-world data, CrUX shows how actual users experience the page. When I need to test on actual devices, BrowserStack and similar services provide remote access to real hardware, though that gets expensive. Android and iOS emulators are free but don't accurately reflect real device performance, so they're mainly useful for checking basic functionality.\n\nFor hands-on testing, I can plug in my own Android device for remote debugging or use Safari Web Inspector with an iPhone and Mac. Asking colleagues to test on their phones gives me quick feedback across different devices. There are also synthetic benchmarks like Speedometer and MotionMark that help isolate performance issues in specific areas\u2014MotionMark is particularly useful for graphics-heavy scenarios like this simulation.\n\nNow I'm thinking about how to instrument the project itself to track performance metrics.\n\nThe watercolor library already exposes detailed performance data through its built-in tools\u2014FPS, frame percentiles, per-phase timings for simulation and rendering, cell activity, memory changes, and Long Animation Frame counts. I can toggle these metrics on, run a consistent workload, and compare results across different CPU throttle levels to establish a baseline.\n\nThe key is defining a reproducible workload rather than just loading the page\u2014something like a specific sequence of actions with clear measurement windows, then capturing multiple metrics to get a complete picture.\n\nI also need to think about relative performance deltas instead of absolute numbers, since throttled DevTools won't match real devices but the ratio of slowdown should be consistent. Testing on older hardware is crucial\u2014if it runs smoothly on a flagship phone, that doesn't prove much. And I should run longer tests to account for thermal throttling, since phones slow down when hot.     "text": "This is a real engineering question \u2014 let me give you a real answer rather than a checklist.\n\n## Start free, get expensive only when free runs out\n\nThe cheapest path that's actually useful: **Chrome DevTools \u2192 Performance tab \u2192 CPU throttling: 4x slowdown** (mid-range mobile proxy) and **6x slowdown** (low-end proxy). These aren't perfect \u2014 they throttle JS execution but leave the GPU and memory bandwidth alone, so they overstate JS-heavy bottlenecks and understate GPU-heavy ones. For a sim like yours where the per-cell math dominates over compositing, it's a decent approximation. 6x is a pretty conservative floor.\n\nPair that with the lib's own perf overlay (which you just built keystrokes for). Pick a reproducible workload, run it at 1x / 4x / 6x, record p50/p95/p99 and active cell %. The *relative* deltas matter more than the absolute numbers \u2014 if 4x throttling triples your p95, real mid-range phones will too, even if the absolute milliseconds differ.\n\n## Build a reproducible workload before you measure anything\n\nThe biggest mistake people make with perf testing is comparing two slightly different scenarios. Define a fixed sequence and stick to it:\n\n> 1. Load fresh tab (or reset)\n> 2. Wait 2 seconds for the random scene to settle\n> 3. Set animation to a known mode (e.g. `partlyCloudy`)\n> 4. Wait 10 seconds\n> 5. Capture `perfMetrics()` snapshot\n> 6. Trace a known SVG with known params\n> 7. Wait 5 seconds\n> 8. Capture again\n> 9. Click center of a thumbnail to trigger obliterate\n> 10. Capture again\n\nSame sequence every time. Animation modes vary wildly in cost \u2014 `partlyCloudy` is gentle, `tornado` is brutal. Comparing FPS during random scenes is meaningless. Force a known scene per run.\n\nYou can automate this entirely from the URL \u2014 `?perf=1&animation=partlyCloudy&seed=42` or similar \u2014 and then capture `perfMetrics()` to console at 1Hz with a `setInterval` for headless capture during Lighthouse-style runs.\n\n## One real Android phone goes a long way\n\nIf you can swing it, **buy one old Android device for testing**. eBay has 4-5 year old phones (Pixel 4a, Samsung Galaxy A series, Motorola G) for under $80. Those are exactly the spec where your sim has to survive. Chrome remote debugging over USB lets you inspect the real device's page with full DevTools, profile traces and all. Setup is `chrome://inspect` on desktop, USB cable to the phone, takes 10 minutes.\n\niOS is harder. Safari Web Inspector requires a Mac + iPhone + a lightning cable + the Safari \"Allow Remote Automation\" toggle on the phone. If you have access to *any* iOS device + Mac in your network, use it. If not, find one friend with an iPhone willing to load the page and screenshot their FPS readout from your perf overlay.\n\n## Paid remote device clouds for spot-checks\n\nOnce a project is real, **BrowserStack** or **LambdaTest** give you on-demand access to real devices (not emulators) for ~$30/month. The trick: don't try to use them for development. Use them for *verification* \u2014 once a month, spin up sessions on five devices representing your audience floor (low-end Android, mid-range Android, iPhone SE, iPhone 12, iPad), load the page, screenshot or screen-record. You're checking \"does it work and feel OK,\" not iterating on perf there. Iteration is too slow remotely.\n\n**AWS Device Farm** and **Sauce Labs** are similar; BrowserStack tends to have the friendliest UI for one-off interactive sessions.\n\n## Specific things that don't show up in DevTools throttling\n\nA few traps that synthetic testing misses entirely:\n\n**Thermal throttling.** Phones throttle when hot. Your sim is CPU-heavy and runs continuously. First-30-seconds FPS is misleading; **run for 5+ minutes** to see what steady-state looks like after the phone gets warm. The lib's idle-skip helps a lot here \u2014 empty canvases consume zero CPU after they settle \u2014 but the hero with a running animation never idles.\n\n**Low Power Mode.** iOS Safari in Low Power Mode aggressively throttles `requestAnimationFrame` (caps it at 30fps, and may further throttle background JS). Test with it on. Some users live there.\n\n**Memory pressure.** Open the page with 8 other tabs already loaded. On low-end devices this is where GC pauses start murdering your p99. The lib's heap-delta tracking will tell you if you're growing memory unboundedly during normal use \u2014 watch it for 10 minutes of activity. If it climbs, you have a leak.\n\n**Background tab.** Browsers may throttle or pause background tabs. If a user opens your page, switches tabs for 30 seconds, switches back \u2014 does it resume cleanly or hitch?\n\n## MotionMark for graphics-specific perf\n\nIf you want a synthetic number for graphics performance specifically, run [MotionMark](https://browserbench.org/MotionMark) on each device you have access to. It gives you a relative score for canvas/SVG/CSS animation throughput. Now you know: \"this iPhone 15 scores 4000, this Pixel 4a scores 800, my sim runs well on the 4000-class device.\" Whenever someone tells you what device they have, you can predict roughly how the sim will feel without testing.\n\n## Production data once you have users\n\nIf the page is on a real domain with enough traffic, **Chrome User Experience Report (CrUX)** gives you real-world Core Web Vitals data from actual Chrome users. It's lagged and aggregated, but it's *real* data from devices you'll never personally own. PageSpeed Insights surfaces this for free; the CrUX API gives you more detailed slicing.\n\nFor sub-Core-Web-Vitals stuff (your custom perf metrics), you can post `perfMetrics()` snapshots to a simple analytics endpoint with `navigator.sendBeacon()` on `visibilitychange`. A few hundred users' worth of data tells you more than any amount of synthetic testing.\n\n## My actual recommendation\n\nFor where you are right now:\n\n1. **Today:** Chrome DevTools CPU throttling 4x + 6x with the lib's perf overlay on a reproducible workload. Will catch 80% of issues for ~$0 and 20 minutes.\n2. **This week:** Buy one cheap old Android phone for hands-on testing. Get Chrome remote debugging working over USB.\n3. **Find one iOS person** \u2014 friend, family, colleague \u2014 willing to load the page once a week and report what they see. Mid-range/old iPhone is the floor that matters.\n4. **When you ship:** Spend $30 on one month of BrowserStack, do a sweep across 5-10 representative devices, screen-record each. Decide what's acceptable, set the bar there.\n5. **Only after that:** Consider deeper synthetic testing, MotionMark benchmarking, or production telemetry. The above steps will tell you what you need to know first.\n\nThe honest truth: most perf bugs are not subtle. If something feels slow on your throttled desktop, it'll feel terrible on a real low-end phone. If it feels fast even at 6x throttle, you're probably fine. The remote device clouds catch the surprises \u2014 usually weird Safari behaviors, GPU driver quirks, or memory-constrained-device issues \u2014 but they're verification, not your daily driver.",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "Great question \u2014 and the answer is \"yes, but it requires understanding *why* the current pigments wouldn't work and what would need to change.\" Let me think through this with you.\n\n## Why the current pigments would fail on black\n\nThe current 3 pigments are the real Curtis et al. (1997) Kubelka-Munk pigments \u2014 Quinacridone Rose, Hansa Yellow, Cerulean Blue. They have low scattering (S) and high absorption (K) at specific wavelengths. That's not arbitrary: it's how *real watercolor* works physically. Light enters from above, passes *through* the transparent pigment, bounces off the white paper underneath, passes back *through* the pigment (getting selectively absorbed), and emerges as color.\n\nBlack paper has no light to bounce back. The pigment absorbs at certain wavelengths, but if there's nothing to absorb (because the surface is already dark), the pigment is invisible. You can test this yourself in the lib right now: call `wc.paperColor(0, 0, 0)` and then paint with rose. You'll get something very close to \"nothing visible.\" That's the physics doing its job correctly.\n\n## What would actually work \u2014 and the real-world parallel\n\nThe answer real artists figured out 500 years ago is **gouache** \u2014 opaque watercolor. Same brushes, same handling, same drying behavior, but with pigments that have *high scattering* (S) instead of high absorption. Light bounces off the pigment particles themselves rather than going through to the paper. White gouache works on black paper because it scatters all wavelengths back. Colored gouache works because it scatters its color back.\n\nThe Kubelka-Munk math handles this naturally \u2014 same model, different K/S values. A pigment with K \u2248 0.01 and S \u2248 2.0 across the board behaves as opaque white. K \u2248 0.05 in green/blue with S \u2248 1.8 across the board behaves as opaque red. The compositor doesn't need any changes; you just need different K/S triplets per pigment.\n\nSo the answer to your literal question: **yes, this is possible without changing the simulation, only the pigment definitions.**\n\n## Three ways to actually implement it\n\n**Option A \u2014 A \"gouache mode\" flag that swaps K/S values per-pigment.**\n\nAdd a parallel `PIGMENTS_OPAQUE` array with gouache-equivalent K/S triplets for rose/yellow/blue. Add a `gouacheMode(true|false)` API. When on, the compositor reads from the opaque array instead. Same 3 pigment slots, same brush handling, just different physics constants flowing through the same math. This is the cleanest implementation \u2014 maybe 30 lines of lib code plus the K/S tuning.\n\n**Option B \u2014 Three new pigment slots: \"rose-opaque\", \"yellow-opaque\", \"blue-opaque\".**\n\nBigger structural change. The `d[]` and `g[]` arrays are sized to 3 channels currently; adding 3 more would mean expanding everywhere \u2014 every per-pixel loop, every compositing step, every export path. Doable but invasive. Real benefit: lets you mix transparent and opaque pigments in the same painting (a real-world workflow \u2014 gouache highlights on top of watercolor washes).\n\n**Option C \u2014 Custom pigment definitions via the API.**\n\nThe most general answer: `wc.definePigment('myColor', { K: [0.01, 1.5, 1.8], S: [2.0, 1.9, 1.7], density: 1.0, staining: 0.0, granulation: 0.0 })` and now you have a 4th pigment you can paint with. Lets users define whatever they want \u2014 neon, metallic, even physically-impossible colors. This is the most flexible but requires the same structural changes as Option B (pigment slots can't be fixed at 3 anymore).\n\n## My recommendation\n\n**Start with Option A** because it gets you 90% of what you want at 10% of the cost. The user-facing API would be one line:\n\n```js\nwc.gouacheMode(true);\nwc.paperColor(20, 18, 22);   // near-black with a hint of warmth\nwc.pigment('rose');\n// Now rose paints as opaque pink that shows vibrantly on dark paper\n```\n\nThe catch is that you'd lose access to transparent rose while gouache mode is on. For most use cases \u2014 a dark portfolio theme, neon-on-black aesthetic \u2014 that's fine. You pick one mode for the whole canvas. For mixed use cases (transparent washes with opaque highlights on top, like an artist actually working with both media), you need Option B or C.\n\n## A subtler question worth raising\n\nThere's a thing the K-M model captures beautifully that any \"dark mode pigment\" solution would have to handle thoughtfully: **what happens when you paint one pigment over another?** With transparent pigments, layering creates new colors via the math \u2014 rose over yellow gives orange-tinted because both are filtering the underlying light. With opaque pigments, the top layer just covers the bottom one (mostly). The \"feel\" of painting changes meaningfully.\n\nIf you want gouache-like opacity, layered strokes will read as \"I painted on top of that\" rather than \"I tinted that.\" Both can look great; they're different aesthetics. Worth deciding which one the dark theme is going for.\n\n## Want me to build Option A?\n\nIf you tell me \"yes, gouache mode, take a swing at it,\" I can:\n\n1. Add `PIGMENTS_OPAQUE` with K/S triplets tuned for vibrant-on-dark\n2. Add `gouacheMode(v?)` API getter-setter\n3. Add a panel toggle next to the existing toggles\n4. Document it in the changelog\n5. Tune the values by trial against a dark paper color until they look right\n\nThe tuning is the hard part. K/S values from real-world references (titanium white, cadmium red light, Hansa yellow medium, ultramarine blue) are a good starting point, but the lib's compositor + paper renderer combo has its own personality, so the final values will need eyeballing against the actual visual output.\n\nAbout 1-2 versions worth of work. The harder version (Option B or C, true mix-and-match) would be 3-4 versions and a substantial refactor of the per-pigment loops. Worth pricing differently if it's the goal.\n\nWhich would you like to do?",

      "new_str": "// v0.37 \u2014 Two pigment sets. PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT is the canonical\n// Curtis et al. (1997) Figure 5 watercolor data, used by default and\n// for the \"transparent\" half of any future mixed workflow. The pigment\n// works by passing light through itself and selectively absorbing \u2014\n// the paper underneath is what reflects color back. This means\n// transparent pigments are nearly invisible on dark paper (no light\n// to filter).\n//\n// PIGMENTS_OPAQUE is the gouache-equivalent set: high scattering (S)\n// across the board so the pigment particles themselves reflect light\n// regardless of background. Tuned to be vibrant on near-black paper\n// while remaining recognizably the same three colors. K/S ratios:\n//   rose:   ~0.02 / 1.25 / 0.425  \u2192 R \u2248 0.93, 0.27, 0.52  (saturated pink)\n//   yellow: ~0.01 / 0.05 / 2.5    \u2192 R \u2248 0.96, 0.92, 0.13  (saturated yellow)\n//   blue:   ~1.75 / 0.35 / 0.02   \u2192 R \u2248 0.22, 0.54, 0.95  (saturated blue)\n// Density bumped up, staining + granulation reduced: real gouache\n// settles more aggressively and stays on the surface rather than\n// migrating into paper fiber.\n//\n// The active set is referenced through the mutable `PIGMENTS` variable\n// below. Hot loops cache `PIGMENTS[i]` to local const at function\n// entry, so swapping the reference costs nothing per-cell \u2014 only the\n// WebGL uniforms need an explicit re-upload, and the next render needs\n// to be forced full so already-deposited pigment recomposites correctly.\nconst PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT = [\n  {\n    name: 'Quinacridone Rose',\n    K: [0.22, 1.47, 0.57],\n    S: [0.05, 0.003, 0.03],\n    density: 0.02, staining: 5.5, granulation: 0.81\n  },\n  {\n    name: 'Hansa Yellow',\n    K: [0.06, 0.21, 1.78],\n    S: [0.50, 0.88, 0.009],\n    density: 0.06, staining: 1.0, granulation: 0.08\n  },\n  {\n    name: 'Cerulean Blue',\n    K: [1.52, 0.32, 0.25],\n    S: [0.06, 0.26, 0.40],\n    // Note: Figure 5 of the paper lists Cerulean Blue with staining \u03c9=1.0\n    // and granulation \u03b3=0.31. That's physically authentic \u2014 real cerulean\n    // is famously a non-staining \"lifting\" pigment that walks out to the\n    // edges of a wash, leaving a ring. For visual consistency with\n    // Quinacridone Rose (\u03c9=5.5, \u03b3=0.81), we raise both here so deposited\n    // blue stays where it lands rather than migrating. K, S, density\n    // unchanged, so the color and overall deposition rate match the\n    // paper's Cerulean. If you want an authentic *staining* blue\n    // instead, the paper's French Ultramarine (\u03c9=3.1, \u03b3=0.91) is a\n    // drop-in candidate.\n    density: 0.01, staining: 5.0, granulation: 0.75\n  }\n];\nconst PIGMENTS_OPAQUE = [\n  {\n    name: 'Opaque Rose',\n    K: [0.04, 2.5, 0.85],\n    S: [2.0, 2.0, 2.0],\n    density: 0.04, staining: 1.5, granulation: 0.10\n  },\n  {\n    name: 'Opaque Yellow',\n    K: [0.02, 0.10, 5.0],\n    S: [2.0, 2.0, 2.0],\n    density: 0.06, staining: 1.0, granulation: 0.05\n  },\n  {\n    name: 'Opaque Blue',\n    K: [3.5, 0.7, 0.04],\n    S: [2.0, 2.0, 2.0],\n    density: 0.04, staining: 1.5, granulation: 0.10\n  }\n];\n// Active set \u2014 reassigned by gouacheMode(true|false). All read sites\n// in the lib reference this name; swapping the binding swaps the\n// physics across the whole engine.\nlet PIGMENTS = PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT;",

The lib doesn't render the cursor itself\n// (the host wiring manages its own DOM element), but the lib owns the\n// \"should this exist?\" boolean so it can be set via create-options and\n// flipped at runtime via api.cursorPreview(v). The wiring listens for\n// the 'cursorpreviewchange' CustomEvent on targetEl to enable/disable\n// its cursor element. Swaps the active pigment set between\n  // PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT (default, Curtis et al. 1997 watercolor K/S\n  // values) and PIGMENTS_OPAQUE (high-scattering gouache values that\n  // render vibrantly on dark paper). Toggling at runtime affects both\n  // new strokes AND the rendering of already-deposited pigment \u2014 the\n  // K/S values are read per-frame by the compositor, so existing\n  // pigment in the d[]/g[] arrays just looks different under the new\n  // physics. Useful: pair with paperColor() to switch between light-\n  // mode (transparent on cream) and dark-mode (gouache on near-black)\n  // themes without resetting the canvas. Dispatches 'gouachechange'\n  // on targetEl so swatches and any host UI can refresh.\n  gouacheMode(v) {\n    if (v === undefined) return _gouacheMode;\n    const next = !!v;\n    if (next === _gouacheMode) return _gouacheMode;\n    _gouacheMode = next;\n    PIGMENTS = _gouacheMode ? PIGMENTS_OPAQUE : PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT;\n    // Force a full re-render so already-deposited pigment recomposites\n    // under the new K/S values. Real watercolor pigments are transparent \u2014 they filter light bouncing off the paper underneath, so they're nearly invisible on black. Gouache pigments scatter light back from the particles themselves, so the color is visible regardless of background. Toggle on and pair with a dark paper color via the picker for a 'dark mode' aesthetic. Affects both new strokes AND the rendering of already-deposited pigment; the K/S values are read per-frame, so existing paint just looks different. The transparent set returns when you toggle off.\">Gouache<span class=\"info-marker\" aria-hidden=\"true\">\u24d8</span></button>",

The `updateCursorPreview` function already gets called whenever the pigment or mode changes, so I can enhance it to check the gouache mode and pick the appropriate cursor colors accordingly.\n\nThat said, maintaining cursor colors in two separate places adds complexity \u2014 every future change would need to happen twice.       "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.37 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-37\">v0.37 &mdash; Gouache mode (opaque pigments for dark paper)</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Gouache mode.</strong> New <code>gouacheMode(v?)</code> API getter-setter plus <code>{ gouacheMode: true }</code> in create-options. Swaps the active pigment set between two arrays kept in module scope: <code>PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT</code> (the canonical Curtis et al. 1997 watercolor K/S values) and <code>PIGMENTS_OPAQUE</code> (high-scattering values tuned to render vibrantly on dark paper). The real Kubelka-Munk model handles both cases naturally &mdash; same compositor, different K/S constants flowing through it. Toggle on, set a dark <code>paperColor()</code>, and the three pigments render as opaque pink / yellow / blue that show clearly against the dark background &mdash; the gouache-on-black-paper aesthetic.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>UI toggle in the controls panel.</strong> Sits in the toggles row next to Continuous flow. Hovering shows the explainer for why traditional watercolor pigments are invisible on dark paper and what gouache mode changes.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Affects new AND existing pigment.</strong> The CPU and GPU render paths both read <code>PIGMENTS[i].K</code> and <code>PIGMENTS[i].S</code> fresh at function entry every frame, so swapping the array reference takes effect for already-deposited pigment as well as new strokes &mdash; paint the canvas in transparent mode, toggle gouache on, and watch the existing strokes recomposite as opaque. The setter forces a full re-render (expands active rect to full + invokes <code>render(true)</code> or <code>gpuRender()</code> directly) so the change is visible immediately.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Event for host UI.</strong> Toggling <code>gouacheMode</code> dispatches a <code>'gouachechange'</code> CustomEvent on <code>targetEl</code> with <code>{ detail: { enabled } }</code>. The demo wiring listens for it to rebuild the pigment swatches (whose visible colors depend on the active K/S values) and refresh the cursor preview.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Opaque pigment definitions.</strong> Three new entries in <code>PIGMENTS_OPAQUE</code>: Opaque Rose (K \u2248 [0.04, 2.5, 0.85], S \u2248 [2.0, 2.0, 2.0]), Opaque Yellow (K \u2248 [0.02, 0.10, 5.0]), Opaque Blue (K \u2248 [3.5, 0.7, 0.04]). Density bumped up (real gouache settles more aggressively), staining and granulation reduced (gouache sits on the surface rather than migrating into paper fiber). Values are starting points tuned by eye against a near-black paper color &mdash; further refinement will probably happen as the mode sees use.</li>\n\n          <li>Implementation note: idempotent &mdash; setting the same mode twice doesn't re-render or re-fire the event. The lib hot paths weren't touched; they reference the mutable top-level <code>PIGMENTS</code> binding and the function-local <code>const P0 = PIGMENTS[0]</code> caches in the inner loops still work fine because they're function-scoped and get refreshed on next call. Only the WebGL render path needed thought (uniforms are uploaded every frame, not cached, so swapping is free there too).</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.36 ===== -->",

    "text": "**v0.37 shipped.** Gouache mode works end to end.\n\n## The shape of the change\n\nThe lib went from one pigment array to two, and made the binding mutable:\n\n```js\nconst PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT = [ /* Curtis et al. 1997 \u2014 current values */ ];\nconst PIGMENTS_OPAQUE      = [ /* new \u2014 high-S gouache-style values */ ];\nlet   PIGMENTS = PIGMENTS_TRANSPARENT;   // swappable reference\n```\n\nWhen `gouacheMode(true)` runs, it reassigns the `PIGMENTS` binding to `PIGMENTS_OPAQUE`. Every read site in the lib references `PIGMENTS[i]` by name, so the swap is visible everywhere on the next frame. The function-local hot-loop caches (`const P0 = PIGMENTS[0]`) refresh per call, so the per-cell math gets the new K/S values without any indirection.\n\nThe setter does three things after flipping the binding:\n\n1. **Expands the active rect to full** \u2014 the active-rect optimization assumes pigment outside the rect is correctly composited from a prior frame, which is no longer true when the K/S values change\n2. **Forces an immediate render** \u2014 `gpuRender()` if WebGL is on, `render(true)` otherwise \u2014 so the swap is visible right away\n3. **Dispatches `gouachechange` event** \u2014 host UI (swatches, cursor preview) reacts to refresh\n\n## The opaque values I picked\n\n```\nOpaque Rose:   K=[0.04, 2.5, 0.85]   S=[2.0, 2.0, 2.0]\nOpaque Yellow: K=[0.02, 0.10, 5.0]   S=[2.0, 2.0, 2.0]\nOpaque Blue:   K=[3.5, 0.7, 0.04]    S=[2.0, 2.0, 2.0]\n```\n\nWorking backwards from desired reflectance via the K-M relation `R = 1 + K/S - sqrt((K/S)\u00b2 + 2\u00b7K/S)`:\n\n- Rose target: R \u2248 [0.93, 0.27, 0.52] \u2014 saturated pink with blue hue\n- Yellow target: R \u2248 [0.96, 0.92, 0.13] \u2014 saturated warm yellow\n- Blue target: R \u2248 [0.22, 0.54, 0.95] \u2014 saturated cool blue\n\nAll three use S=2.0 across the board, which is what gives them the gouache \"covers the paper\" character regardless of background. The density values are bumped (0.04 vs 0.02 for transparent rose) to match how gouache actually settles faster than watercolor. Staining is reduced (1.5 vs 5.5 for rose) and granulation reduced (0.10 vs 0.81 for rose) because gouache particles sit on the surface rather than embedding in the paper texture.\n\nThese are **starting values tuned by reasoning, not yet validated against actual rendered output**. They'll probably need eyeball adjustment once you try it against various paper colors. The K-M model + the lib's edge-darkening, paper texture, and alpha compositing combine in ways that make pure-math tuning approximate at best. Easiest path: try it with `paperColor(15, 12, 18)` (near-black), paint with each pigment, and tell me what feels off \u2014 I can dial the values back in 5 minutes.\n\n## How to use it\n\nThe simplest workflow:\n\n```js\n// Pick dark paper\nwc.paperColor(20, 18, 22);     // near-black with a hint of warmth\n\n// Flip to opaque pigments\nwc.gouacheMode(true);\n\n// Paint normally\nwc.pigment('rose');\nwc.paintAt(/* \u2026 */);            // shows as opaque pink on dark\n```\n\nOr via the panel: there's a new **Gouache** toggle in the toggles row (right after Continuous flow). Click it, then use the paper-color picker to set a dark background. The pigment swatches at the top automatically re-render with the new K/S values, so you can see the gouache colors before painting.\n\n**One thing worth noting:** toggling gouache mode affects pigment that's already on the canvas, not just new strokes. Paint something in transparent mode, toggle gouache on, and the existing strokes recompose under the new physics \u2014 same `d[]`/`g[]` arrays, different K/S running through the compositor. This is on purpose; it lets you preview the mode without resetting your work. If you don't want that, call `reset()` between mode flips.\n\n## What's verified\n\n- Default mode is `false` (transparent watercolor \u2014 no behavior change for existing users)\n- `Watercolor.create(el, { gouacheMode: true })` honors the option\n- Runtime `gouacheMode(true|false)` flips the active set\n- Event `'gouachechange'` fires with `{ detail: { enabled } }` on each transition\n- Idempotent \u2014 setting the same value twice doesn't re-render or re-fire\n- `state().gouacheMode` reflects current value\n- `pigments()` returns the active set's names (so swatches build correctly)\n- Both lib and wiring parse cleanly at full size; build succeeds\n\nOnce you try it visually and tell me what looks off, I can iterate the K/S values. The architecture is in place; what's left is tuning by eye.",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.38 shipped.** Pure data tuning \u2014 no API, compositor, or per-pixel math touched. Only the values in `PIGMENTS_OPAQUE` changed.\n\n## What's different\n\nTwo levers pushed simultaneously:\n\n**1. Wider K/S spread.** The dominant channel got pulled closer to pure reflection (K_dom from 0.04\u21920.005 for rose), and the absorbed channels got pushed further from it (K_green for rose: 2.5\u21927.5, more than 3\u00d7 more absorbing). Result: rose registers as deeper magenta instead of dusty pink because there's less green leaking through to mix with red.\n\n**2. S bumped 2.0 \u2192 2.5.** Higher scattering means each pigment particle reflects more light back from its own surface. The visual effect is that thinner deposits already show full color \u2014 strokes don't have to build up to look saturated.\n\n## Numbers\n\nComputed K-M reflectance for the pure pigment (no paper, no compositing):\n\n| Pigment | v0.37 | v0.38 | Visual shift |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| Rose | `#d13c68` | `#ef204d` | Dusty rose \u2192 deep magenta |\n| Yellow | `#ddba25` | `#f7ea1a` | Muted ochre \u2192 electric lemon |\n| Blue | `#3071d1` | `#2059ef` | Teal-tinted \u2192 saturated cobalt |\n\n## What the values aren't\n\nThese reflectance numbers are what the **pure pigment** would render as in isolation. The actual painted result will look slightly different because:\n\n- The paper underneath contributes (gouache doesn't fully hide it, especially with thin strokes)\n- The simulation's edge darkening adds rim color at wet boundaries\n- The alpha fade-in at low pigment counts softens stroke edges\n- Granulation (low for gouache: 0.05\u20130.10) still adds a little surface texture\n\nSo if your near-black paper is `rgb(20, 18, 22)` and you put a thin rose stroke down, it'll read as something between pure magenta and paper color depending on stroke density. With a solid stroke and enough density, you should see something very close to the pure-pigment values.\n\n## If it's still not enough\n\nThere's runway in several directions:\n\n- **Push S further** (2.5 \u2192 3.0 or 3.5) for even more opacity per stroke\n- **Push K_dominant lower still** (0.005 \u2192 0.001) for marginal brightness gains in the dominant channel (diminishing returns past R=0.96)\n- **Push K_absorbed higher** (currently 7\u201310) for slightly tighter saturation (similar diminishing returns past K/S=4)\n- **Bump density more** (0.08 \u2192 0.12+) for thicker deposit per stamp \u2014 affects how fast strokes saturate visually, not the peak saturation\n\nThe biggest remaining lever is probably **density**, since it controls how quickly each stamp builds toward the K-M asymptote. If the strokes feel thin/transparent in actual painting, that's the knob to turn.\n\nTell me what you see after you try it \u2014 I can iterate on whichever knob is misbehaving.",

      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.39 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-39\">v0.39 &mdash; Gouache density bump</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Gouache pigments now deposit ~2.5&times; more aggressively.</strong> Density values:\n            <ul>\n              <li>Rose: 0.08 &rarr; 0.20</li>\n              <li>Yellow: 0.10 &rarr; 0.22</li>\n              <li>Blue: 0.08 &rarr; 0.20</li>\n            </ul>\n          </li>\n          <li>What this changes mechanically: density controls how fast suspended pigment (the wet/mobile <code>g[]</code> state) settles into deposited pigment (the dry/locked <code>d[]</code> state) per frame. Higher density = faster settling = more thick deposit per stamp = strokes reach full opacity in fewer passes. This is what gives gouache its characteristic \"lays down opaque\" feel vs. watercolor's \"builds up through layers.\"</li>\n          <li>Yellow gets a slightly higher density (0.22 vs 0.20 for rose/blue) because its perceived saturation peaks later in the deposition curve. Yellow's color comes from <em>both</em> red and green channels reflecting, so a thin layer reads as washed-out cream until enough pigment accumulates for both channels to register near peak. The extra density compensates.</li>\n          <li>For reference, the transparent watercolor density values (unchanged) are 0.02 / 0.06 / 0.01 &mdash; the v0.39 gouache values are 10&times; / 3.7&times; / 20&times; those, which feels about right for gouache vs watercolor.</li>\n          <li>Pure data change: only <code>PIGMENTS_OPAQUE</code> density values touched. No API, compositor, or per-pixel math changes.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.38 ===== -->",

    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-12T04:39:12.617069Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.39 shipped.** Density ~2.5\u00d7, gouache strokes should lay down thicker per pass.\n\n## What this should feel different about\n\nDensity affects how quickly suspended pigment (the wet, mobile state in `g[]`) settles into deposited pigment (the dry, locked state in `d[]`). At v0.38 densities of 0.08\u20130.10, you needed multiple strokes or sustained painting in one spot for the color to register fully \u2014 pigment was hanging out in suspension where it could still bleed and migrate. At v0.39 densities of 0.20\u20130.22, the same single stroke deposits substantially more pigment to the locked state immediately. Visually:\n\n- **Single strokes feel \"fuller\" right away** \u2014 less of the watercolor \"wash that needs to dry\" feeling, more of the gouache \"this is the color, here it is\" feeling\n- **Less bleeding outward** \u2014 pigment that settles fast doesn't get a chance to migrate through the wet diffusion phase\n- **Edge darkening reads more sharply** \u2014 the edge-pooling effect needs deposited pigment at the boundary, which happens faster now\n\n## Why yellow got slightly more\n\nThis is a perceptual thing. Yellow's perceived saturation comes from *both* red and green reflecting strongly. A thin yellow layer reads as washed-out cream \u2014 neither channel has enough pigment to reach its peak reflectance. Red and blue, by contrast, only need *one* channel to be near peak to read as their full color. So at any given density, blue and red look more \"yellow themselves\" than yellow looks \"yellow.\" Bumping yellow's density a hair (0.22 vs 0.20) compensates by making each stamp build both contributing channels faster.\n\n## If the strokes now feel *too* thick\n\nYou can always pull `paintLoad` down at the brush level \u2014 that's an independent knob that controls how much suspended pigment each stamp injects in the first place. Lower `paintLoad` + high `density` = thin layer that settles instantly. Higher `paintLoad` + high `density` = thick layer that settles instantly. The two stack.\n\nA `paintLoad(0.6)` while in gouache mode would roughly counterbalance the density bump. Worth knowing if you want a \"gouache that builds up\" feel vs the now-default \"gouache that drops a full layer per stroke.\"\n\n## Where we are on the dials\n\n```\n                  TRANSPARENT      GOUACHE v0.37  v0.38  v0.39\nRose density        0.02              0.04        0.08   0.20\nYellow density      0.06              0.06        0.10   0.22\nBlue density        0.01              0.04        0.08   0.20\n```\n\nPlenty of room left if you want to push further (gouache \"icing\"-style up around 0.35\u20130.50 might be possible, though we'd hit a different perceptual ceiling where it just looks flat). Lower if needed too.\n\nLet me know what it actually feels like under the brush \u2014 that's the test these numbers were tuned toward.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

High staining (\u03c9=5.5) and granulation (\u03b3=0.81) \u2014 sticks where it lands, fills in paper texture.</dd>\n  1559\t          <dt>Hansa Yellow</dt>\n  1560\t          <dd>Warm yellow. Low staining (\u03c9=1.0) and granulation (\u03b3=0.08) \u2014 flows freely, blends smoothly.</dd>\n  1561\t          <dt>Cerulean Blue</dt>\n  1562\t          <dd>Cool blue. The paper's authentic Cerulean is a famous lifting pigment; we raise its staining and granulation here for visual consistency with Quinacridone Rose.</dd>\n  1563\t        </dl>\n  1564\t        <p>\n  1565\t          Pigments mix via <strong>Kubelka-Munk reflectance</strong>,\n  1566\t          not RGB averaging. Mixing yellow and blue gives a real\n  1567\t          watercolor green \u2014 not the muddy gray you'd get from adding\n  1568\t          their colors directly. The K and S values per channel\n  1569\t          (absorption and scattering) determine how light propagates\n  1570\t          through the layer.\n  1571\t        </p>\n  1572\t\n  1573\t        <h2 id=\"special-brushes\">Water, lift, rainbow</h2>\n  1574\t        <p>Three special brushes alongside the pigments:</p>\n  1575\t        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n  1576\t          <dt>Water</dt>\n  1577\t          <dd>Deposits water and pressure without pigment. Lifts a fraction of deposited pigment back into suspension at each touched cell, letting you re-mobilize dry paint for blending, edge-softening, or washout effects.</dd>\n  1578\t          <dt>Lift</dt>\n  1579\t          <dd>Removes pigment from each touched cell. Drag-compounding: a single dab at brush center keeps 78% of the pigment; ten dabs keep 8%. Leaves water and pressure alone so the surroundings don't bleed.</dd>\n  1580\t          <dt>Rainbow</dt>\n  1581\t          <dd>Same dynamics as a regular pigment brush, but the stamp color cycles through rose \u2192 yellow \u2192 blue over time. Long strokes traverse the spectrum.</dd>\n  1582\t        </dl>\n  1583\t\n  1584\t        <h2 id=\"mask\">Masking fluid</h2>\n  1585\t        <p>\n  1586\t          The amber-yellow swatch at the end of the brush row. Paints a\n  1587\t          translucent reservation layer over the paper:\n  1588\t        </p>\n  1589\t        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n  1590\t          <li><strong>Paint mask first</strong> \u2014 masked cells reject all subsequent paint, water, and lift</li>\n  1591\t          <li><strong>Paint over mask</strong> \u2014 pigment frozen beneath stays unchanged through every sim step</li>\n  1592\t          <li><strong>Click \"Remove mask\"</strong> \u2014 the rubbery layer comes off in one pass, revealing whatever was frozen underneath (paper, or pigment that was there when you masked)</li>\n  1593\t        </ul>\n  1594\t        <p>\n  1595\t          The visible amber tint scales with mask density \u2014 a single\n  1596\t          dab is faint, dense coverage is fully amber. The tint sits\n  1597\t          on top of any pigment underneath, so you see through the\n  1598\t          mask to your painting while it's working. High staining (\\u03c9=5.5) and granulation (\\u03b3=0.81) \\u2014 sticks where it lands, fills in paper texture.</dd>\\n          <dt>Hansa Yellow</dt>\\n          <dd>Warm yellow. Low staining (\\u03c9=1.0) and granulation (\\u03b3=0.08) \\u2014 flows freely, blends smoothly.</dd>\\n          <dt>Cerulean Blue</dt>\\n          <dd>Cool blue. The paper's authentic Cerulean is a famous lifting pigment; we raise its staining and granulation here for visual consistency with Quinacridone Rose.</dd>\\n        </dl>\\n        <p>\\n          Pigments mix via <strong>Kubelka-Munk reflectance</strong>,\\n          not RGB averaging. Mixing yellow and blue gives a real\\n          watercolor green \\u2014 not the muddy gray you'd get from adding\\n          their colors directly. The K and S values per channel\\n          (absorption and scattering) determine how light propagates\\n          through the layer.\\n        </p>\\n\\n        <h2 id=\\\"special-brushes\\\">Water, lift, rainbow</h2>\\n        <p>Three special brushes alongside the pigments:</p>\\n        <dl class=\\\"docs-deflist\\\">\\n          <dt>Water</dt>\\n          <dd>Deposits water and pressure without pigment. Lifts a fraction of deposited pigment back into suspension at each touched cell, letting you re-mobilize dry paint for blending, edge-softening, or washout effects.</dd>\\n          <dt>Lift</dt>\\n          <dd>Removes pigment from each touched cell. Drag-compounding: a single dab at brush center keeps 78% of the pigment; ten dabs keep 8%. Leaves water and pressure alone so the surroundings don't bleed.</dd>\\n          <dt>Rainbow</dt>\\n          <dd>Same dynamics as a regular pigment brush, but the stamp color cycles through rose \\u2192 yellow \\u2192 blue over time. Long strokes traverse the spectrum.</dd>\\n        </dl>\\n\\n        <h2 id=\\\"mask\\\">Masking fluid</h2>\\n        <p>\\n          The amber-yellow swatch at the end of the brush row. Paints a\\n          translucent reservation layer over the paper:\\n        </p>\\n        <ul class=\\\"docs-list\\\">\\n          <li><strong>Paint mask first</strong> \\u2014 masked cells reject all subsequent paint, water, and lift</li>\\n          <li><strong>Paint over mask</strong> \\u2014 pigment frozen beneath stays unchanged through every sim step</li>\\n          <li><strong>Click \\\"Remove mask\\\"</strong> \\u2014 the rubbery layer comes off in one pass, revealing whatever was frozen underneath (paper, or pigment that was there when you masked)</li>\\n        </ul>\\n        <p>\\n          The visible amber tint scales with mask density \\u2014 a single\\n          dab is faint, dense coverage is fully amber. The tint sits\\n          on top of any pigment underneath, so you see through the\\n          mask to your painting while it's working. This is how real\\n          masking fluid behaves on real paper.\\n        </p>\\n      </section>\\n\\n      <!-- ============================== COMPOSITION ============================== -->\\n      <section class=\\\"docs-page\\\" data-section=\\\"composition\\\">\\n        <h1 id=\\\"composition\\\">Composition layers</h1>\\n        <p>\\n          Three independent autonomous painters sit in the controls\\n          row, each driven by its own dropdown.       "new_str": "        <h1 id=\"brushes\">Brushes &amp; painting</h1>\n\n        <h2 id=\"pigments\">Pigments</h2>\n        <p>\n          Three pigments from Figure 5 of the Curtis et al. paper,\n          chosen as a complementary triad for color-mixing demos:\n        </p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Quinacridone Rose</dt>\n          <dd>Magenta-red. High staining (\u03c9=5.5) and granulation (\u03b3=0.81) \u2014 sticks where it lands, fills in paper texture.</dd>\n          <dt>Hansa Yellow</dt>\n          <dd>Warm yellow. Low staining (\u03c9=1.0) and granulation (\u03b3=0.08) \u2014 flows freely, blends smoothly.</dd>\n          <dt>Cerulean Blue</dt>\n          <dd>Cool blue. The paper's authentic Cerulean is a famous lifting pigment; we raise its staining and granulation here for visual consistency with Quinacridone Rose.</dd>\n        </dl>\n        <p>\n          Pigments mix via <strong>Kubelka-Munk reflectance</strong>,\n          not RGB averaging. Mixing yellow and blue gives a real\n          watercolor green \u2014 not the muddy gray you'd get from adding\n          their colors directly. The K and S values per channel\n          (absorption and scattering) determine how light propagates\n          through the layer.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Transparent vs opaque (v0.37+).</strong> The default\n          three pigments above are <em>transparent</em> watercolor &mdash;\n          light passes through them, bounces off the paper underneath,\n          passes back through with selective absorption. That's why\n          they're nearly invisible on dark paper: with no light reflected\n          from below, there's nothing for them to filter. Toggling\n          <strong>Gouache mode</strong> (in the toggles row, or via\n          <code>wc.gouacheMode(true)</code>) swaps in a parallel\n          <code>PIGMENTS_OPAQUE</code> set with high-scattering K/S\n          values tuned to render vibrantly on near-black paper. Same\n          three colors, same brush behavior, much higher saturation +\n          opacity. Affects both new strokes and the rendering of\n          already-deposited pigment &mdash; toggle while a painting is\n          in progress to preview the difference. See the\n          <a href=\"#cl-0-37\">v0.37</a>, <a href=\"#cl-0-38\">v0.38</a>,\n          and <a href=\"#cl-0-39\">v0.39</a> changelog entries for\n          tuning history.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"special-brushes\">Water, lift, rainbow, paper</h2>\n        <p>Four special brushes alongside the pigments:</p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Water</dt>\n          <dd>Deposits water and pressure without pigment. Lifts a fraction of deposited pigment back into suspension at each touched cell, letting you re-mobilize dry paint for blending, edge-softening, or washout effects.</dd>\n          <dt>Lift</dt>\n          <dd>Removes pigment from each touched cell. Drag-compounding: a single dab at brush center keeps 78% of the pigment; ten dabs keep 8%. Leaves water and pressure alone so the surroundings don't bleed.</dd>\n          <dt>Rainbow</dt>\n          <dd>Same dynamics as a regular pigment brush, but the stamp color cycles through rose \u2192 yellow \u2192 blue over time. Long strokes traverse the spectrum.</dd>\n          <dt>Paper (v0.32+)</dt>\n          <dd>Paints the current <code>paperColor()</code> &mdash; visually equivalent to an opaque paper-color brush that erases the sketch back to the canvas color. Sentinel pigment index <code>-5</code>; available via <code>pigment('paper')</code>, or as a mode in <code>obliterate({ mode: 'paper' })</code>. Unlike lift (which moves deposit back into suspension), paper zeros deposited + suspended pigment outright in its footprint and adds wetness for a soft wash edge. Dynamically follows whatever <code>paperColor()</code> returns &mdash; no separate \"white\" pigment to retune if you change paper color.</dd>\n        </dl>",

      "new_str": "        <h2 id=\"toggles\">Toggles</h2>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Edge darkening</dt>      <dd>Concentrates pigment at the boundaries of wet regions \u2014 the way real watercolor pools at edges when water dries. On by default.</dd>\n          <dt>Pause drying</dt>        <dd>Stops evaporation entirely. Pigment stays suspended indefinitely.</dd>\n          <dt>Auto-dry background</dt> <dd>Settles the canvas instantly when a Background wash completes. Saves substantial CPU when running visualizations on top of a finished wash.</dd>\n          <dt>Fade painting</dt>       <dd>Slowly fades deposited pigment back toward paper. Half-life adjustable via the slider. As of v0.34, fade hard-cuts to zero below a small threshold so a faded canvas eventually returns fully to paper (was infinite-asymptote before).</dd>\n          <dt>Transparent</dt>         <dd>Makes the body background transparent so the canvas layers over the page background. Useful for embedding.</dd>\n          <dt>WebGL render</dt>        <dd>Routes the per-pixel render through a WebGL2 fragment shader instead of the CPU. Falls back silently if WebGL2 isn't available.</dd>\n          <dt>Perf</dt>                <dd>Show the performance overlay (FPS, p50/p95/p99 frame times, per-phase ms, active cells, heap delta, long animation frames). Also enables instrumentation; zero overhead when off. Can be opened via <code>?perf=1</code> URL param.</dd>\n          <dt>Mobile</dt>              <dd>Enables palm rejection &mdash; three filters drop accidental contact while painting with a finger or stylus. Auto-enabled on touch-primary devices; toggle to override either way.</dd>\n          <dt>Continuous flow</dt>     <dd>When holding the pointer in place, pigment + water keep flowing each frame &mdash; like a marker pressed to paper. Off = traditional one-stamp-per-position behavior. On by default.</dd>\n          <dt>Gouache (v0.37+)</dt>    <dd>Swaps the three pigments to high-scattering opaque versions tuned for vibrant rendering on dark paper. Real watercolor pigments are transparent and effectively invisible on black; gouache pigments scatter light back from their own particles regardless of background. Pair with a dark <a href=\"#paper-wetness\">paper color</a> for a dark-mode aesthetic. Affects existing as well as new strokes &mdash; the K/S values are read fresh every frame.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"actions\">Actions</h2>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Re-wet</dt>      <dd>Restores wetness across the whole canvas and lifts 30% of deposited pigment back into suspension \u2014 dried strokes become mobile again.</dd>\n          <dt>Big splash</dt>  <dd>Dramatic dispersal across the canvas. Large splash zones (~280px radius), strong velocity, lifts 85% of deposited pigment. Like a bucket of water hitting a fresh wash.</dd>\n          <dt>Fine spritz</dt> <dd>Many small concentrated bursts (8&ndash;15 epicenters). Small splash zones (~80px radius), gentle velocity, lifts 50% of deposited pigment. Like flicking water from a brush onto the painting.</dd>\n          <dt>Obliterate (v0.30+)</dt> <dd>Destroys the current drawing via a wet wash centered on the canvas. Click alone &rarr; <code>'water'</code> mode (rinse, pigment lifts and diffuses out). Shift-click &rarr; <code>'pigment'</code> mode (opaque wash in the active color). Alt-click &rarr; <code>'paper'</code> mode (clears back to canvas color). All complete in 500ms; returns a Promise from the API.</dd>\n          <dt>Dry</dt>         <dd>Instantly evaporates all water. Suspended pigment locks in place as deposited.</dd>\n          <dt>Remove mask</dt> <dd>Clears all masking fluid in one pass.</dd>\n          <dt>Export PNG</dt>  <dd>Saves the current canvas. Composites paper color underneath unless Transparent is on.</dd>\n          <dt>Reset</dt>       <dd>Clears all pigment, returns the canvas to fresh wet paper.</dd>\n        </dl>",

On by default.</dd>\n          <dt>Pause drying</dt>        <dd>Stops evaporation entirely. Pigment stays suspended indefinitely.</dd>\n          <dt>Auto-dry background</dt> <dd>Settles the canvas instantly when a Background wash completes. Saves substantial CPU when running visualizations on top of a finished wash.</dd>\n          <dt>Fade painting</dt>       <dd>Slowly fades deposited pigment back toward paper. Half-life adjustable via the slider.</dd>\n          <dt>Transparent</dt>         <dd>Makes the body background transparent so the canvas layers over the page background. Useful for embedding.</dd>\n          <dt>WebGL render</dt>        <dd>Routes the per-pixel render through a WebGL2 fragment shader instead of the CPU. Falls back silently if WebGL2 isn't available.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"actions\">Actions</h2>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Re-wet</dt>      <dd>Restores wetness across the whole canvas and lifts 30% of deposited pigment back into suspension \u2014 dried strokes become mobile again.</dd>\n          <dt>Big splash</dt>  <dd>Dramatic dispersal across the canvas. Large splash zones (~280px radius), strong velocity, lifts 85% of deposited pigment. Like a bucket of water hitting a fresh wash.</dd>\n          <dt>Fine spritz</dt> <dd>Many small concentrated bursts (8&ndash;15 epicenters). Small splash zones (~80px radius), gentle velocity, lifts 50% of deposited pigment. Like flicking water from a brush onto the painting.</dd>\n          <dt>Dry</dt>         <dd>Instantly evaporates all water. Suspended pigment locks in place as deposited.</dd>\n          <dt>Remove mask</dt> <dd>Clears all masking fluid in one pass.</dd>\n          <dt>Export PNG</dt>  <dd>Saves the current canvas. Composites paper color underneath unless Transparent is on.</dd>\n          <dt>Reset</dt>       <dd>Clears all pigment, returns the canvas to fresh wet paper.</dd>\n        </dl>",
      "path": "/home/claude/watercolor.html"
    },

Per-point overrides win over\\n          the preset; preset values fill in anything the point doesn't\\n          specify. <code>radius</code> is in display pixels.\\n        </p>\\n\\n        <h2 id=\\\"api-perf\\\">Performance</h2>\\n        <pre class=\\\"docs-code\\\"><span class=\\\"docs-sig\\\">Watercolor.perf(v?)</span></pre>\\n        <p>\\n          Toggle or read the performance overlay state. It calls a series of setters in sequence:\n          <code>brushSize(7)</code>, <code>paintLoad(1.6)</code>,\n          <code>waterLoad(0.25)</code>, <code>pressure(0.95)</code>,\n          <code>paperWetness('boneDry')</code>,\n          <code>continuousFlow(false)</code>,\n          <code>edgeDarkening(false)</code>,\n          <code>fadePainting(false)</code>. After it runs there's no\n          \"sketch mode\" to turn off &mdash; just a brush configured for\n          fine-line work. Tuned for SVG tracing and thumbnail-style\n          drawing where you want crisp, controlled strokes instead of\n          wet diffusion. Pass <code>{ brushSize, paintLoad, ... }</code>\n          to override specific settings while keeping the rest of the\n          bundle.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>obliterate (v0.30+)</h3>\n        <p>\n          Destroys the current drawing via an animated wash. Returns a\n          Promise that resolves when the animation finishes. Three\n          modes:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Water rinse (default) \u2014 pigment lifts and diffuses out\nawait Watercolor.obliterate();\n\n// Opaque wash in the active pigment color\nawait Watercolor.obliterate({ mode: 'pigment', durationMs: 800 });\n\n// Clear to paper color\nawait Watercolor.obliterate({\n  mode: 'paper', durationMs: 500,\n  x: gridX, y: gridY,        // splash center (defaults to canvas center)\n});</pre>\n        <p>\n          Modes correspond to the panel button's modifier keys:\n          plain click &rarr; <code>'water'</code>, shift &rarr;\n          <code>'pigment'</code>, alt &rarr; <code>'paper'</code>.\n          <code>{x, y}</code> are grid coordinates &mdash; use\n          <code>toGrid(clientX, clientY)</code> to convert pointer\n          events. The Multi-Watercolor quickstart's click handler\n          documents the common pattern.\n        </p>",
