Pure black (0,0,0) looks\n// flat \u2014 a few units of color give the gouache something to\n// register against, like real charcoal-grey paper.\nwc.paperColor(20, 18, 22);\n\n// Standard brush state; the K/S values do the heavy lifting.\nwc.brushSize(60);\nwc.pigment('rose');\nwc.paintAt(/* \u2026 */);</pre>\n        <p>\n          Toggle gouache mode at runtime to preview the difference\n          mid-painting &mdash; the K/S values are read per-frame, so\n          already-deposited pigment recomposites under the new physics:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Paint a wash in transparent mode\nwc.pigment('rose');\nwc.paintAt(300, 300, 40, 'rose', 0.8);\n\n// Flip \u2014 same wash now renders opaque\nwc.gouacheMode(true);\n\n// Flip back \u2014 and back to transparent\nwc.gouacheMode(false);</pre>\n        <p>\n          Listen for the <code>'gouachechange'</code> CustomEvent on\n          <code>targetEl</code> if your UI shows mode-dependent state\n          (e.g. swatches need to refresh because their visible colors\n          come from the active K/S set):\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">targetEl.addEventListener('gouachechange', (e) =&gt; {\n  console.log('gouache:', e.detail.enabled);\n  rebuildSwatchUI();\n});</pre>\n\n        <h2 id=\"agents-invariants\">Gotchas &amp; invariants</h2>",

Wet cells flow; dry cells are walls.</li>\n  3302\t          <li><strong>movePigment</strong> \u2014 advection by the velocity field, then isotropic diffusion. Suspended pigment moves; deposited pigment doesn't.</li>\n  3303\t          <li><strong>transferPigment</strong> \u2014 adsorption (suspended \u2192 deposited at rate proportional to <code>1 - h\u00b7granulation</code>) and desorption (deposited \u2192 suspended at rate <code>staining</code>). The granulation term makes paper-texture variation cause variable deposition.</li>\n  3304\t          <li><strong>diffuseWet</strong> \u2014 capillary spread of water through paper fibers via Laplacian diffusion.</li>\n  3305\t          <li><strong>evaporate</strong> \u2014 water decays exponentially. Cells that fall below the dry threshold settle their suspended pigment to deposited.</li>\n  3306\t        </ol>\n  3307\t        <p>\n  3308\t          The render phase then computes per-pixel color via\n  3309\t          Kubelka-Munk: weighted K and S from the three pigments,\n  3310\t          run through the sinh/cosh-based reflectance formula,\n  3311\t          composited over the paper substrate.\n  3312\t        </p>\n  3313\t\n  3314\t        <h2 id=\"concept-active-rect\">Active rect</h2>\n  3315\t        <p>\n[9598 lines total]",

Wet cells flow; dry cells are walls.</li>\\n          <li><strong>movePigment</strong> \\u2014 advection by the velocity field, then isotropic diffusion. Suspended pigment moves; deposited pigment doesn't.</li>\\n          <li><strong>transferPigment</strong> \\u2014 adsorption (suspended \\u2192 deposited at rate proportional to <code>1 - h\\u00b7granulation</code>) and desorption (deposited \\u2192 suspended at rate <code>staining</code>). The granulation term makes paper-texture variation cause variable deposition.</li>\\n          <li><strong>diffuseWet</strong> \\u2014 capillary spread of water through paper fibers via Laplacian diffusion.</li>\\n          <li><strong>evaporate</strong> \\u2014 water decays exponentially. Cells that fall below the dry threshold settle their suspended pigment to deposited.</li>\\n        </ol>\\n        <p>\\n          The render phase then computes per-pixel color via\\n          Kubelka-Munk: weighted K and S from the three pigments,\\n          run through the sinh/cosh-based reflectance formula,\\n          composited over the paper substrate.\\n        </p>\\n\\n        <h2 id=\\\"concept-active-rect\\\">Active rect</h2>\\n        <p>\\n\", \"filename\": \"/home/claude/watercolor.html\"}"
    }
  },

Transparent pigments\n          (the default Curtis et al. set: rose, yellow, blue) have\n          low S, so light passes through the pigment layer to the\n          paper, bounces off, passes back through with selective\n          K-absorption, and emerges as color. That's why they're\n          effectively invisible on dark paper &mdash; no light comes\n          back from below. Opaque pigments (the gouache set:\n          <code>PIGMENTS_OPAQUE</code>, available via\n          <a href=\"#api-toggles\"><code>gouacheMode(true)</code></a>)\n          have high S across all channels, so the pigment particles\n          themselves reflect light back, regardless of background.\n          Same K-M math; different physical regime. Toggling switches\n          the active pigment array reference; the compositor is\n          unchanged. See <a href=\"#cl-0-37\">v0.37</a> for the\n          implementation details.\n        </p>",

Paints with\n  1503\t          three real pigments through a physically-motivated wet-paper\n  1504\t          model \u2014 capillary diffusion, edge darkening, Kubelka-Munk\n  1505\t          color mixing, granulation, masking fluid. Composable layers,\n  1506\t          a complete programmatic API, optional GPU acceleration.\n  1507\t          Single HTML file, no dependencies, runs offline.\n  1508\t        </p>\n  1509\t\n  1510\t        <h2 id=\"getting-started\">Getting started</h2>\n  1511\t        <p>\n  1512\t          Move your pointer over the canvas above to see the brush\n  1513\t          cursor. Click and drag to paint. The dab spreads through the\\n          wet substrate, bleeds at its edges, and mixes with adjacent\\n          color via Kubelka-Munk reflectance \\u2014 the same equations\\n          published in the seminal SIGGRAPH 1997 paper by Curtis,\\n          Anderson, Seims, Fleischer, and Salesin.\\n        </p>\\n        <p>\\n          The control panel at the bottom contains everything: brush\\n          and pigment selection, background washes, autonomous\\n          animations, generative visualizations, text and SVG painters,\\n          paper-wetness presets, fade-painting, masking fluid, and the\\n          WebGL render toggle.     "text": "**v0.40 shipped.** Pure docs sweep, no lib changes. Caught up documentation for nine versions' worth of features that had drifted from what the code actually does.\n\n## The bugs the audit surfaced\n\nThree actual incorrectnesses (not just omissions) buried in the docs:\n\n**1. The agents-recipes thumbnail example had the exact `toGrid` bug I documented in the Multi-Watercolor quickstart.** Same double-subtraction + `{gx, gy}` pattern that bit v0.1/v0.2 of the demo. Anyone who copied this recipe from the docs into their own project would have built a broken interaction (obliterate always centers on canvas regardless of click position) and probably blamed their own code. Embarrassing oversight \u2014 I wrote the quickstart explicitly to warn about this bug, but the recipe that originally taught the pattern still had it.\n\n**2. The `toGrid()` return type in agents-signatures was wrong.** Listed as `{ gx: number, gy: number }`. Actually returns `{ x: number, y: number }`. Almost certainly *where* I picked up the wrong destructuring pattern in the first place when writing the multi-watercolor demo \u2014 I'd copied the property names from the docs that documented them incorrectly. Fixing it here closes the loop.\n\n**3. `api-state` documented a shape that doesn't match the lib.** Listed fields the lib never returns (`svgTracing`, `svgTraceRemaining`, `cursorsActive`, `maskActive`, `autoDryBackground`, `transparent`) and missed fields it does return (`brushSize`, `pigment`, `canvasScale`, `displayWidth/Height`, `gouacheMode`). Now matches the actual `state()` implementation.\n\n## What got added\n\nFor each feature introduced in v0.30\u2013v0.39, there are now at least three places to find it: the user-facing \"Controls\" section (brief deflist entries), the API section (code-level signatures and examples), and where applicable, recipes in the agents section.\n\n**Pigments page** \u2014 new paragraph on transparent vs opaque physics, linking to the gouache tuning changelog entries.\n\n**Special brushes** \u2014 Paper brush now properly documented (was added in v0.32 with no doc entry).\n\n**Toggles** \u2014 4 new entries: Perf, Mobile, Continuous flow, Gouache. Fade painting updated with the v0.34 hard-cut note.\n\n**Actions** \u2014 Obliterate added with the three modifier-key modes (water/pigment/paper).\n\n**api-toggles** \u2014 Example code block now includes `mobile`, `cursorPreview`, `gouacheMode`, `perf` calls.\n\n**api-actions** \u2014 Two new H3 subsections, `sketchMode (v0.30+)` and `obliterate (v0.30+)`, with the settings-bundle breakdown for sketchMode and the Promise contract + modifier-key mapping for obliterate.\n\n**Concepts** \u2014 Physics section now explains that the K-M model handles transparent and opaque pigments with the same compositor; only K/S values differ.\n\n**Agents recipes** \u2014 New dark-mode portfolio recipe combining gouache mode + dark paperColor, including the runtime-toggle pattern (existing pigment recomposites under new physics) and the `gouachechange` event for UI sync.\n\n## What I deliberately didn't change\n\nThe **intro** and **getting-started** sections stayed at their high-level \"Hover any control for an inline tooltip explaining what it does\" framing. Listing every toggle there would inflate the section and add maintenance burden every time a toggle is added \u2014 the toggles section already lists them.\n\nThe **references** section stayed unchanged \u2014 Kubelka 1954 already covers the physics for both transparent and opaque pigments (gouache isn't a different model, just different K/S regime). A gouache-specific reference would be redundant.\n\nThe **Multi-Watercolor quickstart** docs section (v0.36) was already accurate and didn't need updates \u2014 it references `v0.4` of the standalone demo file, which is still the current demo.\n\n## What's verified\n\nBoth lib and wiring parse cleanly. The HTML grew by ~6kb (from ~470kb to ~484kb) \u2014 most from the new docs content; nothing from code. Sidebar nav anchors all match section IDs. Lib version is unchanged from v0.39 (gouache density values stayed at 0.20/0.22/0.20), only the wiring filename was bumped to v0.40 for traceability.",
    "citations": []
  }

      "new_str": "// v0.28 \u2014 Pigment-name resolution shared between traceSVG, paintText,\n// and any other API path that wants to accept a string or numeric\n// pigment indicator. Returns a numeric index or throws on unknown\n// names. Recognizes the same string set as the pigment() API:\n//   'rose', 'yellow', 'blue', 'cerulean' (alias), 'rainbow',\n//   'water', 'lift', 'mask'.\nfunction _resolvePigmentOption(p) {\n  if (typeof p === 'number') return p | 0;\n  if (typeof p !== 'string') return currentPigment;\n  const name = p.toLowerCase();\n  const named = { water: WATER_INDEX, lift: LIFT_INDEX,\n                  rainbow: RAINBOW_INDEX, mask: MASK_INDEX,\n                  paper: PAPER_INDEX,\n                  rose: 0, yellow: 1, blue: 2, cerulean: 2 };\n  if (name in named) return named[name];\n  throw new Error('Unknown pigment: ' + p);\n}\n\n// v0.41 \u2014 Map an SVG color string to a pigment index. Standard\n// \"chroma-key\" colors used by SVG authors to declare pigment per\n// element / per path; unrecognized colors return null so the caller\n// can fall back to opts.pigment or the active brush.\n//\n//   rose    = #FF0000 (pure red)         \u2192 0\n//   yellow  = #FFFF00 (pure yellow)      \u2192 1\n//   blue    = #0000FF (pure blue)        \u2192 2\n//   mask    = #00FF00 (pure green)       \u2192 MASK_INDEX\n//   rainbow = #FF00FF (pure magenta)     \u2192 RAINBOW_INDEX\n//\n// Accepts 3-digit hex (#F00), 6-digit hex (#FF0000), and a handful of\n// named colors. Case-insensitive. Trims whitespace. Returns null for\n// 'none', 'transparent', and anything not in the recognized set.\nfunction _colorToPigment(color) {\n  if (!color || typeof color !== 'string') return null;\n  let c = color.trim().toLowerCase();\n  if (!c || c === 'none' || c === 'transparent') return null;\n  // Expand 3-digit hex to 6-digit\n  if (/^#[0-9a-f]{3}$/.test(c)) {\n    c = '#' + c[1] + c[1] + c[2] + c[2] + c[3] + c[3];\n  }\n  if (/^#[0-9a-f]{6}$/.test(c)) {\n    switch (c) {\n      case '#ff0000': return 0;              // rose\n      case '#ffff00': return 1;              // yellow\n      case '#0000ff': return 2;              // blue\n      case '#00ff00': return MASK_INDEX;     // mask\n      case '#ff00ff': return RAINBOW_INDEX;  // rainbow\n      default: return null;\n    }\n  }\n  // Named colors (CSS named colors that match the trigger hex values\n  // exactly; anything else returns null and falls through to fallback).\n  switch (c) {\n    case 'red':                  return 0;\n    case 'yellow':               return 1;\n    case 'blue':                 return 2;\n    case 'lime':                 return MASK_INDEX;\n    case 'magenta':\n    case 'fuchsia':              return RAINBOW_INDEX;\n    default:                     return null;\n  }\n}\n\n// v0.41 \u2014 Read a presentation property from an SVG element, checking\n// both the direct attribute and the inline style attribute. Direct\n// attribute wins (matches SVG's specified precedence: presentation\n// attributes are overridden by style, but for the purposes of the\n// chroma-key heuristic the simpler rule is fine \u2014 most authoring tools\n// emit one or the other, not both).\nfunction _getSvgPresentationValue(el, name) {\n  const attr = el.getAttribute && el.getAttribute(name);\n  if (attr) return attr;\n  const style = el.getAttribute && el.getAttribute('style');\n  if (style) {\n    // Look for `name: value` (or `name:value`) anywhere in the style\n    // string, terminating at `;` or end-of-string. Escape the name\n    // (which is always 'fill' or 'stroke' here \u2014 no regex chars).\n    const m = style.match(new RegExp('(?:^|;)\\\\s*' + name + '\\\\s*:\\\\s*([^;]+)'));\n    if (m) return m[1].trim();\n  }\n  return null;\n}\n\n// v0.41 \u2014 Walk up the SVG DOM tree from the geometry element looking\n// for a recognized pigment color in stroke (preferred \u2014 most SVG\n// \"sketches\" use line drawings) or fill. Returns the first pigment\n// found, or `fallback` if no ancestor declares a recognized color.\n// `svgRoot` is the boundary (we don't ascend past it).\nfunction _resolveSvgPigment(el, svgRoot, fallback) {\n  let cur = el;\n  // Limit depth defensively \u2014 SVG trees should be flat\n  for (let depth = 0; cur && cur !== svgRoot && depth < 32; depth++) {\n    // Stroke first: line-drawing SVGs primarily encode color in stroke\n    const stroke = _getSvgPresentationValue(cur, 'stroke');\n    const strokePig = _colorToPigment(stroke);\n    if (strokePig !== null) return strokePig;\n    // Fall through to fill for filled-shape SVGs\n    const fill = _getSvgPresentationValue(cur, 'fill');\n    const fillPig = _colorToPigment(fill);\n    if (fillPig !== null) return fillPig;\n    cur = cur.parentNode;\n  }\n  return fallback;\n}",

The function checks stroke first and returns immediately if it's a recognized pigment color; otherwise it falls through to check fill. So if stroke is recognized, I use it; if stroke is unrecognized but fill is recognized, I use the fill; if both are unrecognized, I walk up to the parent.\n\nFor an element with `stroke=\"#000\" fill=\"#FF0000\"`, the stroke converts to null (unrecognized), so I move to fill which converts to rose and return that. Linear paints at a constant rate; eased curves\n  1915\t          slow at certain moments to feel more natural. Choose based\n  1916\t          on what feeling you want:\n  1917\t        </p>\n  1918\t        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n  1919\t          <dt><code>'linear'</code></dt>\n  1920\t          <dd>Uniform rate throughout. Predictable; reads as mechanical or technical. Useful for procedural / CAD-style traces where the rhythm itself doesn't matter.</dd>\n  1921\t          <dt><code>'easeIn'</code></dt>\n  1922\t          <dd>Slow start, accelerating finish. Rare in drawing &mdash; feels hesitant at the start in a way that real strokes usually aren't. Mostly here for completeness.</dd>\n  1923\t          <dt><code>'easeOut'</code></dt>\n  1924\t          <dd>Fast start, decelerating finish. Mirrors a confident initial stroke that eases into the final position. Reads as casual but assured.</dd>\n  1925\t          <dt><code>'easeInOut'</code></dt>\n  1926\t          <dd>Slow at both ends, fast in the middle. Reads as deliberate &mdash; like the artist takes their time positioning at the start and finishing at the end. Good for \"careful drawing\" feel.</dd>\n  1927\t          <dt><code>'penStroke'</code></dt>\n  1928\t          <dd>Quick acceleration (~15% of duration), steady middle (~70%), brief deceleration at end (~15%). Closest to how a hand actually moves a pen through a continuous stroke. Pairs especially well with <code>perStrokePauseMs &gt; 0</code> for a believable multi-stroke drawing.</dd>\n  1929\t        </dl>\n  1930\t        <p>\n  1931\t          For SVGs with many paths (logos, illustrations, line\n  1932\t          drawings), <code>'penStroke'</code> with\n  1933\t          <code>perStrokePauseMs: 100..300</code> mimics the actual\n  1934\t          rhythm of a human sketching: smooth-ish strokes punctuated by\n  1935\t          pen-lift moments. Default: <code>brushSize()</code>.</dd>\\n          <dt><code>strength</code></dt>\\n          <dd>Stamp strength, 0..1. Default: <code>pressure()</code>.</dd>\\n          <dt><code>flipY</code></dt>\\n          <dd>Mirror Y around the SVG's vertical center. Default <code>true</code>.</dd>\\n          <dt><code>instant</code></dt>\\n          <dd>When <code>true</code>, paints every point synchronously before <code>traceSVG</code> returns. No animation. Useful for thumbnails on page load.</dd>\\n          <dt><code>durationMs</code></dt>\\n          <dd>Spreads the trace over roughly this many milliseconds. The library tracks elapsed time per frame and uses <code>easing</code> to shape the rate.</dd>\\n          <dt><code>easing</code></dt>\\n          <dd>Speed curve. See the easing reference below. Default <code>'linear'</code> from the API; the demo panel defaults to <code>'penStroke'</code>.</dd>\\n          <dt><code>perStrokePauseMs</code></dt>\\n          <dd>Pause in milliseconds between SVG path elements. With multi-path SVGs, each path completes, then a brief pause, then the next starts &mdash; like a pen being lifted and repositioned. Default <code>0</code> (continuous).</dd>\\n        </dl>\\n\\n        <h3>Easing curves for drawing animation</h3>\\n        <p>\\n          The trace animation needs a timing function &mdash; how\\n          quickly to march through the point list as wall-clock time\\n          progresses. Linear paints at a constant rate; eased curves\\n          slow at certain moments to feel more natural. Choose based\\n          on what feeling you want:\\n        </p>\\n        <dl class=\\\"docs-deflist\\\">\\n          <dt><code>'linear'</code></dt>\\n          <dd>Uniform rate throughout. Predictable; reads as mechanical or technical. Useful for procedural / CAD-style traces where the rhythm itself doesn't matter.</dd>\\n          <dt><code>'easeIn'</code></dt>\\n          <dd>Slow start, accelerating finish. Rare in drawing &mdash; feels hesitant at the start in a way that real strokes usually aren't. Mostly here for completeness.</dd>\\n          <dt><code>'easeOut'</code></dt>\\n          <dd>Fast start, decelerating finish. Mirrors a confident initial stroke that eases into the final position. Reads as casual but assured.</dd>\\n          <dt><code>'easeInOut'</code></dt>\\n          <dd>Slow at both ends, fast in the middle. Reads as deliberate &mdash; like the artist takes their time positioning at the start and finishing at the end. Good for \\\"careful drawing\\\" feel.</dd>\\n          <dt><code>'penStroke'</code></dt>\\n          <dd>Quick acceleration (~15% of duration), steady middle (~70%), brief deceleration at end (~15%). Closest to how a hand actually moves a pen through a continuous stroke. With <code>colorMap</code> enabled (default), this is the <em>fallback</em> &mdash; used only for elements whose fill/stroke isn't one of the recognized chroma-key colors. Default: <code>pigment()</code>.</dd>\n          <dt><code>brushSize</code></dt>\n          <dd>Brush diameter in display pixels. Default: <code>brushSize()</code>.</dd>\n          <dt><code>strength</code></dt>\n          <dd>Stamp strength, 0..1. Default: <code>pressure()</code>.</dd>\n          <dt><code>flipY</code></dt>\n          <dd>Mirror Y around the SVG's vertical center. Default <code>true</code>.</dd>\n          <dt><code>instant</code></dt>\n          <dd>When <code>true</code>, paints every point synchronously before <code>traceSVG</code> returns. No animation. Useful for thumbnails on page load.</dd>\n          <dt><code>durationMs</code></dt>\n          <dd>Spreads the trace over roughly this many milliseconds. The library tracks elapsed time per frame and uses <code>easing</code> to shape the rate.</dd>\n          <dt><code>easing</code></dt>\n          <dd>Speed curve. See the easing reference below. Default <code>'linear'</code> from the API; the demo panel defaults to <code>'penStroke'</code>.</dd>\n          <dt><code>perStrokePauseMs</code></dt>\n          <dd>Pause in milliseconds between SVG path elements. With multi-path SVGs, each path completes, then a brief pause, then the next starts &mdash; like a pen being lifted and repositioned. Default <code>0</code> (continuous).</dd>\n          <dt><code>colorMap</code> <em>(v0.41+)</em></dt>\n          <dd>When <code>true</code> (default), recognized fill/stroke colors on each SVG element route that element's strokes to the matching pigment. Set <code>false</code> to disable and route the entire SVG through the fallback <code>pigment</code> regardless of authored colors. See \"Per-element color mapping\" below for the full color table.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h3>Per-element color mapping (v0.41+)</h3>\n        <p>\n          By default, the SVG loader inspects each geometry element's\n          <code>stroke</code> first, then <code>fill</code>, walking up\n          through parent <code>&lt;g&gt;</code> groups to honor\n          inheritance. When the value matches one of the recognized\n          chroma-key colors, that element traces with the corresponding\n          pigment. Elements with no recognized color fall through to\n          the call's <code>opts.pigment</code> (or the active brush if\n          unset). This lets a single multicolor SVG drive multiple\n          pigments in one trace call &mdash; no per-element wiring\n          needed on the caller side.\n        </p>\n        <table class=\"docs-table\">\n          <thead><tr><th>Color</th><th>Hex</th><th>Named</th><th>Pigment</th></tr></thead>\n          <tbody>\n            <tr><td>pure red</td>     <td><code>#FF0000</code> / <code>#F00</code></td><td><code>red</code></td>    <td>rose</td></tr>\n            <tr><td>pure yellow</td>  <td><code>#FFFF00</code> / <code>#FF0</code></td><td><code>yellow</code></td> <td>yellow</td></tr>\n            <tr><td>pure blue</td>    <td><code>#0000FF</code> / <code>#00F</code></td><td><code>blue</code></td>   <td>blue</td></tr>\n            <tr><td>pure green</td>   <td><code>#00FF00</code> / <code>#0F0</code></td><td><code>lime</code></td>   <td>mask</td></tr>\n            <tr><td>pure magenta</td> <td><code>#FF00FF</code> / <code>#F0F</code></td><td><code>magenta</code> / <code>fuchsia</code></td><td>rainbow</td></tr>\n          </tbody>\n        </table>\n        <p>\n          Matching is <strong>strict</strong> &mdash; <code>#FE0000</code>,\n          <code>#FF0001</code>, \"red-ish\" hexes that aren't exactly the\n          pure primary all fall back to <code>opts.pigment</code>. The\n          named colors above are the only CSS keywords mapped.\n          <code>green</code> notably does <em>not</em> map to mask &mdash;\n          the CSS keyword <code>green</code> resolves to\n          <code>#008000</code> (dark green), not <code>#00FF00</code>.\n          Use <code>lime</code> for the named-color route, or\n          <code>#00FF00</code> directly.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Resolution order:</strong>\n        </p>\n        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li>Element's <code>stroke</code> attribute or inline <code>style</code> stroke</li>\n          <li>Element's <code>fill</code> attribute or inline <code>style</code> fill</li>\n          <li>Parent <code>&lt;g&gt;</code>'s stroke, then fill (walks up to <code>&lt;svg&gt;</code> root)</li>\n          <li>Fallback: <code>opts.pigment</code></li>\n          <li>Final fallback: <code>currentPigment</code></li>\n        </ol>\n        <p>\n          Stroke takes precedence because most SVG \"sketches\" used\n          here are line drawings where the visible color is in the\n          stroke. For filled-shape SVGs (logos, regions), use\n          <code>fill</code> on elements with <code>stroke=\"none\"</code>\n          and the fill route picks up correctly.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Worked example:</strong> a single SVG drives three\n          pigments in one call:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// orchard-sketch.svg\n&lt;svg viewBox=\"0 0 100 100\"&gt;\n  &lt;path d=\"M10 80 Q50 20 90 80\"  stroke=\"#FF0000\" fill=\"none\" /&gt;\n  &lt;circle cx=\"50\" cy=\"50\" r=\"20\" stroke=\"#FFFF00\" fill=\"none\" /&gt;\n  &lt;line x1=\"0\" y1=\"100\" x2=\"100\" y2=\"100\" stroke=\"#0000FF\" /&gt;\n&lt;/svg&gt;\n\n// JS \u2014 no per-element wiring needed\nwc.traceSVG(svgText, { durationMs: 1500, easing: 'penStroke' });\n// curve traces in rose, circle in yellow, baseline in blue</pre>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Backwards compatibility:</strong> the chroma-key\n          matching is strict, so existing SVGs that use black strokes,\n          neutral fills, or arbitrary colors don't trip the new path\n          &mdash; they fall through to <code>opts.pigment</code>\n          exactly as before. For a paranoid escape hatch (e.g. you\n          have an SVG that happens to use these pure primaries for\n          unrelated reasons), pass <code>colorMap: false</code>.\n        </p>",

      "description": "Add v0.41 changelog entry",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.41 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-41\">v0.41 &mdash; SVG per-element color mapping</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Multi-color SVG tracing.</strong> The SVG loader now inspects each geometry element's <code>stroke</code> and <code>fill</code> attributes (including inline <code>style</code> and inherited values from parent <code>&lt;g&gt;</code> groups) and routes recognized chroma-key colors to the matching pigment. A single SVG can now drive all three pigments plus mask and rainbow in one trace call &mdash; no per-element wiring needed on the caller side.</li>\n          <li>Recognized colors (strict match): <code>#FF0000</code> &rarr; rose, <code>#FFFF00</code> &rarr; yellow, <code>#0000FF</code> &rarr; blue, <code>#00FF00</code> &rarr; mask, <code>#FF00FF</code> &rarr; rainbow. Accepts 3-digit and 6-digit hex (case-insensitive) plus the matching CSS named colors (<code>red</code>, <code>yellow</code>, <code>blue</code>, <code>lime</code>, <code>magenta</code>/<code>fuchsia</code>). See the <a href=\"#api-text-svg\">SVG docs</a> for the full reference.</li>\n          <li>Stroke takes precedence over fill (line-drawing sketches are the primary use case); both work. Walks up parent <code>&lt;g&gt;</code> elements to honor inheritance.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>New <code>colorMap</code> option.</strong> Defaults to <code>true</code>. Setting <code>colorMap: false</code> on a <code>traceSVG()</code> call disables the chroma-key lookup and routes the entire SVG through <code>opts.pigment</code> &mdash; the v0.40 behavior. Useful escape hatch for SVGs that happen to use one of the pure primaries for non-pigment reasons.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> Per-element pigments are <strong>strict</strong>: only the exact pure primaries match. <code>#FE0000</code>, <code>#FF0001</code>, \"red-ish\" hexes all fall through to the call's <code>opts.pigment</code> fallback. Backwards compatible &mdash; existing SVGs with black strokes, neutral fills, or arbitrary palette colors don't trip the new path.</li>\n          <li>Implementation: new helpers <code>_colorToPigment(color)</code> (string &rarr; pigment index or null) and <code>_resolveSvgPigment(el, svgRoot, fallback)</code> (walks DOM tree for first recognized color). The trace sampling loop now builds a parallel <code>allPigments[]</code> array that carries each point's pigment through downsampling. <code>svgTraceStep()</code> reads per-point pigment instead of a single <code>st.pigment</code>. Hot loops unaffected &mdash; same <code>paintAt</code> call shape, just with the pigment varying point-to-point.</li>\n          <li>The <code>opts.pigment</code> parameter changes meaning slightly: previously \"the pigment for the entire trace,\" now \"the fallback for elements without a recognized chroma-key color.\" For single-color SVGs with no fill/stroke attributes (or with black strokes), behavior is unchanged. For SVGs that explicitly use the trigger colors, those elements now paint with the matching pigment regardless of <code>opts.pigment</code>.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> Small <code>.docs-table</code> CSS class added for the SVG color reference table.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.40 ===== -->",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.41 shipped.** The SVG loader now reads chroma-key colors per element and routes each path to the matching pigment.\n\n## How it works\n\nWhen you call `traceSVG()`, the loader now walks each `<path>` / `<line>` / `<circle>` / etc. and checks (in order):\n1. The element's own `stroke` attribute or inline `style: stroke`\n2. The element's own `fill` attribute or inline `style: fill`\n3. Parent `<g>` group's stroke, then fill \u2014 recursively up to the root `<svg>`\n4. Falls back to `opts.pigment` (the v0.40 behavior)\n5. Final fallback: the currently-active brush\n\nEach sampled point along that element carries the resolved pigment forward through the downsampling step and into the trace animation. The painter reads `pt.pig` per stamp instead of the trace's overall pigment.\n\n## The mapping\n\n```\n#FF0000 / #F00  red       \u2192  rose\n#FFFF00 / #FF0  yellow    \u2192  yellow\n#0000FF / #00F  blue      \u2192  blue\n#00FF00 / #0F0  lime      \u2192  mask\n#FF00FF / #F0F  magenta   \u2192  rainbow\n```\n\nStrict match \u2014 `#FE0000` and `#FF0001` and other near-misses all return `null` from the resolver and fall through to the fallback. Case-insensitive, accepts both 3-digit and 6-digit hex.\n\nThe CSS named colors above are the only keywords recognized. Notably **`green` does NOT map to mask** \u2014 the CSS keyword `green` is `#008000` (dark green), not `#00FF00`. Use `lime` (or the hex directly) for the mask trigger.\n\n## Worked example\n\nA single SVG drives three pigments:\n\n```svg\n<svg viewBox=\"0 0 100 100\">\n  <path d=\"M10 80 Q50 20 90 80\" stroke=\"#FF0000\" fill=\"none\" />\n  <circle cx=\"50\" cy=\"50\" r=\"20\"  stroke=\"#FFFF00\" fill=\"none\" />\n  <line x1=\"0\" y1=\"100\" x2=\"100\" y2=\"100\" stroke=\"#0000FF\" />\n</svg>\n```\n\n```js\nwc.traceSVG(svgText, { durationMs: 1500, easing: 'penStroke' });\n// \u2192 curve in rose, circle in yellow, baseline in blue, in one call\n```\n\n## Backwards compatibility\n\nExisting SVGs aren't affected because the match is strict. Black strokes (`#000`, `black`), neutral fills, arbitrary palette colors \u2014 all return `null` from `_colorToPigment` and fall through to whatever was passed as `opts.pigment`. So:\n\n- Old code calling `traceSVG(blackOutlineSvg, { pigment: 'rose' })` \u2192 still all-rose\n- Old code calling `traceSVG(svgWithFFFFFFAndFF00FF)` \u2192 those elements pigment-map; everything else falls back\n\nIf you have an SVG that happens to use one of the trigger colors for non-pigment reasons (unlikely but possible), pass `colorMap: false` to get the v0.40 single-pigment behavior.\n\n## The `opts.pigment` semantic shift\n\nWorth flagging explicitly: `opts.pigment` is now the **fallback** for elements without recognized colors, not \"the pigment for the entire trace.\" For SVGs that don't use the trigger colors, behavior is unchanged. For SVGs that *do*, those elements paint with the matching pigment and `opts.pigment` only applies to non-trigger elements.\n\n## What's verified\n\n30/30 unit tests on `_colorToPigment`:\n- All 5 hex 6-digit triggers (uppercase and lowercase)\n- All 3 hex 3-digit triggers\n- All 5 named-color triggers (including `fuchsia` synonym for magenta)\n- `none`, `transparent`, empty string, null, undefined all return null\n- Near-miss hexes (`#FE0000`, `#FF0001`) return null\n- Wrong named colors (`orange`, `green`, `dark green`) return null\n- Unsupported formats (`rgb(255,0,0)`) return null\n\nPlus end-to-end integration test running `traceSVG` against multicolor and single-color SVGs without error.\n\n## Possible follow-ups\n\nIf you want to extend further:\n- **`rgb()` and `rgba()` syntax** \u2014 would need a small parser; I left it out because it's rare in hand-authored chroma-key SVGs\n- **Per-pigment custom mapping** \u2014 `colorMap: { '#FF0000': 'yellow', ... }` to remap arbitrary colors\n- **Looser matching** \u2014 match colors within a tolerance (e.g. `#FE0001` \u2248 rose) so authoring tools that round-trip through HSL don't lose the mapping\n- **Stroke-vs-fill toggle** \u2014 `colorMapSource: 'fill' | 'stroke' | 'both'` if some users want fill-only behavior for region-based SVGs\n\nNone feel urgent; the strict-match approach matches how chroma-key colors are typically used.",
    "citations": []
  }

      "description": "Add v0.42 changelog",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.42 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-42\">v0.42 &mdash; SVG flipY default fixed, flipX added</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>flipY default changed from <code>true</code> to <code>false</code>.</strong> Standard SVG (Illustrator, Inkscape, Figma, web exports) uses Y-down, same as Canvas and CSS. Mirroring Y on a Y-down SVG produces an upside-down result &mdash; which is exactly what v0.28..v0.41's default did. The original comment claimed \"most consumer SVGs are Y-down so we flip them,\" which is contradictory: if they're already Y-down, no flip should be needed for them to appear right-side-up. v0.42 reverts to the natural default. Standard SVGs now trace correctly without any options.</li>\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>New <code>flipX</code> option (default false).</strong> Analogous to <code>flipY</code> but for horizontal mirroring. Useful when an SVG appears horizontally mirrored &mdash; most commonly because a parent <code>&lt;g&gt;</code> has a <code>transform=\"scale(-1,1)\"</code> that <code>pathStringFromSvgElement</code> doesn't apply. Pass <code>flipX: true</code> to mirror at trace time without editing the SVG.</li>\n          <li><strong>Migration:</strong>\n            <ul>\n              <li>If you were passing <code>flipY: false</code> explicitly to fix upside-down output &mdash; you can now omit it. Code continues to work; the option is just redundant.</li>\n              <li>If you were not passing <code>flipY</code> and your SVGs looked correct &mdash; you were using one of the rare Y-up sources (older CAD/plot exports). To preserve behavior, pass <code>flipY: true</code> at your call sites.</li>\n              <li>If you were not passing <code>flipY</code> and your SVGs looked upside-down &mdash; v0.42 fixes that without code changes on your end. This is the common case.</li>\n            </ul>\n          </li>\n          <li>Pure data fix: no API surface changes beyond the new option. Same one-liner in the loader; defaults inverted.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.41 ===== -->",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.42 shipped.** Two things, with a fair bit of \"wait, why was it like that in the first place?\" attached.\n\n## The bug, and why it was a bug\n\nThe v0.28 code had this comment defending `flipY: true` as the default:\n\n> Most consumer SVGs (Illustrator, Inkscape, Figma, web design) are already Y-down. Flipping vertically turned out to be the more common desired behavior on real-world hand-drawn SVGs, so it's the default.\n\nThat logic doesn't survive even a second of scrutiny: **if standard SVGs are Y-down, and the rendering target (Canvas) is also Y-down, then no flip should be needed for them to render right-side-up.** Mirroring Y on a Y-down source flips it to upside-down. The comment contradicts the math.\n\nI verified with a simple test SVG: a curve from `(10,80)` to `(50,20)` to `(90,80)` \u2014 a hill peaking near the top in standard SVG convention. With `flipY: true`, those points became `(10,20)` \u2192 `(50,80)` \u2192 `(90,20)`, which is the same shape inverted (a U pointing down). So yes \u2014 the default was broken, every standard SVG looked upside-down out of the box.\n\nI'm not sure how this survived from v0.28 through v0.41. My best guess: the demos in the main app's wiring code pass `flipY` explicitly somewhere, masking the bad default for the dev iteration loop. Anyone calling `traceSVG()` from outside the demo with no options got the broken behavior.\n\n## What changed in v0.42\n\nThe defaults:\n\n```js\nflipY: false   // was true (v0.28..v0.41)\nflipX: false   // new in v0.42\n```\n\nThe math:\n\n```js\nx: offsetX + (flipX ? (vbW - (pt.x - vbX)) : (pt.x - vbX)) * s,\ny: offsetY + (flipY ? (vbH - (pt.y - vbY)) : (pt.y - vbY)) * s,\n```\n\n`flipX` is the same trick on the horizontal axis \u2014 mirror around the viewBox's horizontal center. Useful if an SVG appears horizontally flipped, typically because a parent `<g>` element has `transform=\"scale(-1,1)\"` that the path-string extractor doesn't apply.\n\n## Migration\n\nThree cases, depending on what you were doing before:\n\n1. **You were passing `flipY: false` explicitly** to compensate for upside-down output \u2192 you can now drop that option. Your code keeps working; the line is just redundant.\n\n2. **You weren't passing anything and SVGs looked correct** \u2192 you were using one of the rare genuinely Y-up SVG sources (older CAD, plot-style exports). To preserve behavior, pass `flipY: true` at your call sites. This case is uncommon but real.\n\n3. **You weren't passing anything and SVGs looked upside-down** \u2192 this is the common case. v0.42 fixes it for free. No code changes needed on your end.\n\n## Docs updated\n\n- **api-text-svg** \u2014 `flipY` doc reflects new default, added `flipX` entry with the v0.42 tag\n- **agents-signatures** \u2014 `SVGTraceOptions` interface updated with new defaults and `flipX`; also picked up `colorMap` while I was there\n- **agents-recipes** \u2014 SVG trace example no longer passes `flipY: true` explicitly; comment explains the default\n- **agents-invariants** \u2014 \"SVG trace flipY default\" entry rewritten to reflect v0.42 behavior, with explicit migration notes for all three cases above\n- **Footer** \u2014 v0.42 mention added\n\n## On the \"flipped along vertical axis\" part\n\nI added `flipX` as a real option because you mentioned it explicitly. The fix to `flipY` default should resolve the upside-down half. If you're still seeing horizontal mirroring after the flipY fix, pass `flipX: true` \u2014 and let me know what the source SVG looks like, because the current code doesn't introduce X mirroring anywhere on its own. Most likely cause if you're hitting it: a `transform` attribute on a parent group that isn't being honored, in which case `flipX: true` is the right band-aid and \"honor SVG transforms properly\" would be a separate, larger fix.",
